blocks.caption: The Spark UI reveals the bottleneck through progressive drill-down. Jobs show which action is slow. Stages show which stage within that job is slow. Tasks show whether the slowness is uniform (all tasks slow — too much data) or skewed (one task slow — data skew).

blocks.content: The SQL/DataFrame Tab

The SQL/DataFrame tab shows the execution plan as a visual DAG with actual metrics. Each operator node displays the actual number of rows processed, the actual data size, and the actual time spent. This is more useful than explain() because explain() shows estimates while the SQL tab shows actuals. NordGrid's engineers compare the estimated row count from the plan with the actual row count from the SQL tab — a large discrepancy indicates stale statistics that caused Catalyst to make a suboptimal plan choice.

blocks.title: The Spark UI is the performance debugger
blocks.content: The execution plan is the performance specification — it shows what Spark intends to do. The Spark UI is the performance debugger — it shows what Spark actually did, how long each piece took, and where the bottleneck hides. NordGrid's rule: never file a performance complaint without a Spark UI screenshot showing the bottleneck. 'The notebook is slow' is not a diagnosis. 'Stage 5 takes 18 minutes because the shuffle reads 14 GB across 48 partitions with 2.1 GB of disk spill' is a diagnosis that leads directly to a fix.

Catalyst optimizes the execution plan before the query runs, using table statistics and heuristics. But Catalyst's estimates are sometimes wrong — the table statistics are stale, the data distribution has changed, or the filter selectivity is different from what Catalyst predicted. Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) is a runtime optimizer that adjusts the plan during execution based on actual intermediate results. In Fabric, AQE is enabled by default, and its three features — partition coalescing, join conversion, and skew handling — address the three most common mid-level performance problems automatically.
Feature 1: Coalescing Shuffle Partitions

When spark.sql.shuffle.partitions is set to 200 but the actual shuffle output is small (say, 12 rows for a regional groupBy), AQE merges the 200 partitions down to a smaller number at runtime. This eliminates the overhead of scheduling and coordinating 188 empty tasks. You can see this in the Spark UI: the plan shows Exchange with 200 partitions, but the actual execution shows CustomShuffleReader with a reduced partition count. NordGrid's regional aggregation goes from 200 planned partitions to 8 actual partitions after AQE coalescing.
Feature 2: Converting Sort-Merge to Broadcast Join

Catalyst decides the join strategy before execution based on estimated table sizes. If both tables appear large, Catalyst chooses a sort-merge join (two shuffles). But if one table is actually small after filters are applied (the estimate was wrong because statistics were stale), AQE can convert the sort-merge join to a broadcast join at runtime — eliminating both shuffles. NordGrid's enrichment join between filtered Silver meters (15M rows for one month) and the substations reference table (200 rows) was planned as sort-merge because Catalyst estimated the Silver filter would return 500M rows. AQE saw the actual 15M rows and converted to broadcast.

# AQE will convert this sort-merge join to broadcast at runtime
# if the filtered Silver table is small enough after filter evaluation
df_meters = (
    spark.read.table("silver_meters")
    .filter((col("year") == 2025) & (col("month") == 3))
    .select("region", "daily_kw")
)
df_subs = spark.read.table("ref_substations")

df_joined = df_meters.join(df_subs, on="region")
df_joined.explain()
# Plan shows SortMergeJoin (Catalyst's pre-execution choice)
# But actual execution uses BroadcastHashJoin (AQE's runtime optimization)
# Verify in the Spark UI SQL tab: look for 'AQE' annotations on join nodes
Feature 3: Skew Join Optimization

When AQE detects that one partition in a shuffle is significantly larger than others (skew), it splits that partition into smaller sub-partitions and replicates the corresponding partition from the other side of the join to match. This eliminates the single-task bottleneck without requiring manual salting. NordGrid's NORTH-1 region contains 25% of all meters (300K of 1.2M) while other sub-regions average 75K. Without AQE skew handling, the NORTH-1 partition takes 4x longer than any other. With AQE, the NORTH-1 partition is split into 4 sub-partitions that complete in the same time as the others.
Verifying AQE in the Spark UI

The Spark UI's SQL/DataFrame tab shows AQE activity with specific annotations. Look for CustomShuffleReader (coalescing happened), AQEShuffleRead (a shuffle was optimized), and the isFinalPlan=true marker at the top of the plan (AQE has finalized the plan after observing actual statistics). If isFinalPlan=false, AQE has not yet run — the plan is the pre-execution estimate. NordGrid's engineers verify AQE behavior after every pipeline run by checking the SQL tab for these markers. If AQE is not activating when expected, the cause is usually a configuration override that disabled it.

blocks.title: AQE is a safety net, not a strategy
blocks.content: AQE fixes problems that Catalyst could not predict. It does not replace good data engineering practices. A notebook that relies on AQE to coalesce 200 partitions down to 8 every single run is wasting compute on the initial 200-partition shuffle before AQE intervenes. A notebook that sets partition count to 48 from the start avoids the waste entirely. Use AQE as a safety net for unexpected conditions (data volume spikes, stale statistics) and good configuration as the baseline. NordGrid's approach: configure well, let AQE handle the exceptions.

Lessons 2–7 taught the individual tools: execution plans, partition pruning, column pruning, partition count tuning, the Spark UI, and AQE. This lesson combines them into a diagnostic workflow — the step-by-step process NordGrid's engineers follow when a notebook exceeds its SLA. The workflow is designed to find the bottleneck in the minimum number of steps, starting with the cheapest checks (cold start separation, job-level timing) and progressing to deeper investigation (stage metrics, task distribution, plan analysis) only when the cheap checks do not reveal the cause.
The Diagnostic Workflow

items: Separate cold start from execution. Check the notebook's total run time in the monitoring hub. Subtract the Spark session cold start time (typically 30–60 seconds in Fabric). If the cold start is 60 seconds and the total is 180 seconds, the actual computation is only 120 seconds — the problem might be cold start, not the query.

Identify the slowest job. Open the Spark UI Jobs tab. Find the job with the longest duration. In NordGrid's Silver notebook, job 2 (saveAsTable) is always the longest. If job 0 (read) is the longest, the bottleneck is I/O, not computation.
Identify the slowest stage within that job. Click into the job and find the stage with the longest duration. Check the stage's shuffle read/write volumes, input size, and task count.

Check the task duration distribution. If max/median > 5x, the problem is skew — one partition is doing most of the work. If max/median ≈ 1x and all tasks are uniformly slow, the problem is volume — too much data per task.
Check the execution plan. Run explain() on the DataFrame. Verify PartitionFilters (partition pruning active?), ReadSchema (column pruning active?), and join strategy (broadcast or sort-merge?).

Apply the fix based on the diagnosis. Skew → enable AQE skew handling or salt the key. Volume → reduce scan via pruning or increase parallelism. Shuffle → reduce partition count or broadcast small tables. Full scan → add partition column filters.

blocks.content: NordGrid's 47-Minute Notebook: The Diagnosis

Applying the workflow to NordGrid's slow Silver notebook: Step 1: Total time 47 minutes, cold start 45 seconds — the computation is 46 minutes. Step 2: Job 2 (saveAsTable) takes 40 of 46 minutes. Step 3: Stage 5 (the shuffle before the write) takes 28 minutes. The shuffle reads 14 GB. Step 4: Task duration distribution shows max 16 minutes, median 12 seconds — extreme skew. Step 5: The execution plan shows Exchange hashpartitioning(region, 200) — the shuffle partitions by region with 200 partitions. Diagnosis: the NORTH-1 region contains 25% of the data, creating a single massive partition.
The Fix

# Before: shuffle by region with 200 partitions — NORTH-1 gets 25% of data
# Duration: 47 minutes

# Fix 1: Reduce shuffle partitions to match actual parallelism
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", 48)

# Fix 2: Enable AQE skew handling (already enabled by default in Fabric)
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled", "true")

# Fix 3: Add column pruning before the write
df_final = (
    df_clean
    .select("meter_id", "daily_kw", "date", "region", "customer_type",
            "year", "month", "daily_mw", "processing_timestamp")
)

# After: 14 minutes (from 47)
# Breakdown: cold start 45s, read 2min, transform 3min, write 8min, validate 30s

Three fixes combined reduced the notebook from 47 minutes to 14 minutes — a 3.4x improvement that brought it well within the 20-minute SLA. The largest contributor was the partition count reduction (200 to 48) combined with AQE skew handling, which eliminated the 16-minute outlier task. The column pruning contributed a smaller but meaningful reduction by cutting shuffle volume from 14 GB to 6 GB. Total investigation time: 20 minutes. Total fix implementation time: 5 minutes. The measure-diagnose-fix-verify cycle worked.

blocks.title: Most slow notebooks have one bottleneck
blocks.content: In NordGrid's experience, 80% of slow notebooks have a single dominant bottleneck that accounts for 60–80% of the total runtime. Finding that one bottleneck and fixing it provides the majority of the improvement. The diagnostic workflow is designed to find that bottleneck quickly — the first four steps take 5 minutes and identify the problem in most cases. Resist the temptation to apply every optimization technique simultaneously. Fix the biggest bottleneck, measure the result, and decide whether further optimization is needed.

Lessons 2–8 covered Spark performance techniques that apply in any Spark environment — execution plans, pruning, partition tuning, the Spark UI, and AQE. This lesson covers performance patterns that are unique to Fabric's managed Spark environment: capacity throttling that slows notebooks during peak usage, V-Order optimization that trades write speed for read speed, session pool management that eliminates cold start overhead, and the interaction between Spark notebooks and other Fabric workloads sharing the same capacity. These patterns have no equivalent in standalone Spark clusters because they arise from Fabric's SaaS architecture.
Capacity Throttling

Fabric's capacity model allocates compute resources from a shared pool. When the pool is heavily utilized — multiple notebooks running simultaneously, a large Power BI refresh in progress, a Warehouse query consuming significant resources — Fabric throttles lower-priority workloads by queuing them or reducing their executor allocation. NordGrid's production incident: the Silver notebook normally completes in 14 minutes, but on the first Monday of the month it took 38 minutes. The cause was the monthly billing Power BI report refresh running simultaneously, consuming 40% of the F64 capacity. The notebook received fewer executors and each task took longer.

The fix is scheduling discipline: NordGrid staggers the Silver notebook (02:00 UTC), the Gold notebook (02:20 UTC), and the Power BI refresh (04:00 UTC) to avoid overlap on the shared capacity pool. When overlap is unavoidable — for example, ad-hoc analyst queries running during pipeline execution — NordGrid monitors the capacity metrics app for throttling events and adjusts pipeline schedules seasonally. During heavy reporting months (quarter-end), the monthly billing Power BI refresh is moved from 03:00 to 05:00 UTC to give the data engineering pipelines uncontested capacity during their execution window.
V-Order Optimization

V-Order is a Fabric-specific write optimization that sorts data within Parquet files for maximum compression and read performance. When enabled, Delta table writes take slightly longer (5–10% overhead) but the resulting files are smaller and read faster — both by Spark queries and by the SQL analytics endpoint. NordGrid enables V-Order for all Silver and Gold table writes because the tables are written once daily but read dozens of times by analysts, dashboards, and downstream notebooks. The write overhead is amortized across many reads.

# Enable V-Order for Delta table writes
# V-Order sorts data for optimal compression and read performance
# Write takes 5-10% longer, but every subsequent read is faster
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.parquet.vorder.enabled", "true")

# Also enable optimize-write to auto-coalesce small output files
spark.conf.set("spark.microsoft.delta.optimizeWrite.enabled", "true")

df_final.write \
    .mode("overwrite") \
    .partitionBy("year", "month") \
    .saveAsTable("silver_meters")

# Result: ~8% slower write, ~25% faster reads for SQL endpoint queries
# and ~15% better compression (smaller files in OneLake)
Session Pool and Warm Starts

Fabric's Spark session cold start (30–60 seconds) is a fixed overhead that cannot be eliminated but can be reduced. Fabric supports session pooling: pre-warmed Spark sessions that are ready to execute code immediately. When session pooling is enabled in the workspace settings, the first cell execution attaches to a pre-warmed session instead of starting a new one, reducing startup from 45 seconds to under 5 seconds. NordGrid enables session pooling for production workspaces because the 40-second savings per pipeline run accumulates to 200+ minutes per week across their 30+ daily notebook runs.
OneLake Caching

When a Spark job reads from OneLake, the data is cached on the local SSD of the executor node. Subsequent reads of the same data are served from the local cache rather than from OneLake, which is significantly faster. This caching behavior benefits notebooks that read the same table multiple times — NordGrid's Silver notebook reads the Silver table once for transformation and once for validation. The validation read is faster because the data is already cached from the transformation read. However, the cache is per-session and per-executor — a new session or a different executor reads from OneLake again.

blocks.title: Fabric performance is not just Spark performance
blocks.content: In a standalone Spark cluster, performance depends only on Spark configuration and data characteristics. In Fabric, performance depends on those factors plus capacity utilization, workload scheduling, V-Order settings, session pool configuration, and OneLake caching behavior. A notebook that runs in 14 minutes during off-peak hours might take 25 minutes during peak hours because of capacity contention. NordGrid treats Fabric performance as a system concern, not just a Spark concern — and the capacity management module (Module 8) covers the platform-level aspects in depth.

The previous nine lessons covered the tools and techniques for Spark performance in Fabric: execution plans, partition pruning, column pruning, partition count tuning, the Spark UI, AQE, diagnostic workflows, and Fabric-specific patterns. This lesson synthesizes everything into NordGrid's production performance governance — the standards, checklists, and processes that ensure performance is maintained as the pipeline evolves, the data grows, and new engineers join the team. Performance governance is not bureaucracy — it is the mechanism that prevents gradual degradation from eventually breaching SLAs.
SLA Definitions

Headroom is the difference between the SLA and the current performance, expressed as a percentage of the SLA. NordGrid maintains a minimum 20% headroom on all pipelines. When headroom drops below 20% — either because data volume grew or because a code change regressed performance — the pipeline is flagged for optimization. The headroom policy prevents SLA breaches by catching gradual degradation before it crosses the threshold. NordGrid's Silver notebook had 50% headroom at launch (10 minutes vs 20-minute SLA) and gradually consumed it over 6 months as data volume grew. The team optimized when headroom reached 15%.
The Plan Review Checklist

items: PartitionFilters present and correct. Every query that filters by time must show year and month in PartitionFilters. An empty PartitionFilters on a time-filtered query is an automatic rejection.

ReadSchema contains only required columns. Compare the ReadSchema to the output schema. If ReadSchema contains columns that do not appear in the final output, add a select() before the expensive operation.
Join strategy appropriate. Small tables (< 10 MB after filters) should use BroadcastHashJoin. Large tables should use SortMergeJoin. If a small table is using SortMergeJoin, add a broadcast() hint.

Shuffle partition count appropriate. Check spark.sql.shuffle.partitions against the expected output volume. The count should produce 128–256 MB partitions for large outputs, or match the expected group count for small aggregations.

No unexpected full table scans. Every FileScan should have either PartitionFilters or PushedFilters or both. A FileScan with empty filters on a query that has WHERE conditions indicates a pushdown failure.

blocks.content: The Performance Review Process

NordGrid's code review checklist includes a performance section. Before any notebook is promoted from staging to production, the reviewing engineer must verify: the execution plan passes the five-point checklist above, the Spark UI shows no skew (max/median task duration < 5x), the total duration is within the SLA with at least 20% headroom, and the capacity consumption is within the notebook's CU budget. The reviewer does not need to optimize the notebook — they need to verify that the author has already optimized it and documented the baseline metrics.
What Comes Next

Module 2 takes the Delta Lake foundation from the junior-level track and adds the mid-level operations that production requires: MERGE for incremental updates, OPTIMIZE and VACUUM for table maintenance, schema evolution for handling upstream changes, and the interaction between Delta operations and the SQL endpoint and semantic model. The performance skills from this module — reading plans, checking pruning, verifying partition counts — apply directly to Delta operations, where a poorly maintained table (fragmented files, stale statistics) degrades query performance over time.

blocks.title: Performance is a practice, not a project
blocks.content: NordGrid does not optimize once and declare victory. Performance degrades gradually as data grows, schemas evolve, and new queries are added. The five-point checklist runs on every code review. The SLA headroom is tracked monthly. The Spark UI is reviewed after every overnight run. Performance is a continuous practice — like testing and deployment — not a one-time project. The team that treats performance as ongoing maintenance keeps their pipelines healthy. The team that treats it as a project discovers SLA breaches at the worst possible moment.

chapters.title: Advanced Delta Lake Operations
chapters.description: Master Delta Lake's MERGE operation for upserts, implement table maintenance strategies, handle schema evolution in production, and use time travel for debugging and recovery.

blocks.content: In the junior-level track, Delta Lake was a storage format – Parquet files plus a transaction log that enables ACID writes, schema enforcement, and time travel. You wrote tables with saveAsTable(), read them with spark.read.table(), and trusted that the format handled the rest. At 500K rows per day with a full overwrite, that trust was well-placed. At 1.2M rows per day with three years of history (1.3 billion rows, 3.2 TB), the junior-level patterns start breaking. A full overwrite rewrites 3.2 TB every night instead of appending the day’s 3 GB. The transaction log grows to thousands of entries, slowing metadata operations. Small files accumulate from daily writes, degrading scan performance. This module teaches the Delta Lake operations that production requires at mid-level scale.
What Changes at Scale

rows: Full overwrite daily – simple, correct, fast enough
Full overwrite rewrites 3.2 TB nightly – 45 minutes and growing
MERGE (upsert) writes only the changed data – 3 GB instead of 3.2 TB
1,000+ small files accumulate from appends and MERGEs
Small files degrade scan performance – each file requires a separate I/O operation
3,000+ entries from MERGEs, appends, and maintenance
Log scanning slows metadata operations – checkpoint creation becomes necessary
Rare – schema defined once, never changes
Quarterly – upstream systems add columns, change types
Schema evolution must be handled without breaking downstream consumers
Rerun the overwrite – idempotent by nature
MERGE is not idempotent – rerunning can duplicate records
Time travel and transaction rollback become essential recovery tools

blocks.content: The Junior-to-Mid Transition

The transition from junior to mid-level Delta operations is a shift from write-and-forget to write-and-maintain. At junior level, you wrote a table and moved on. At mid-level, you write a table, monitor its file count, compact its small files weekly, vacuum its obsolete versions bi-weekly, handle schema changes when they arrive, and recover from failed writes using time travel. The table is no longer a static artifact – it is a living system that requires ongoing maintenance to stay healthy. NordGrid learned this when their Silver table’s query performance degraded by 40% over six months because nobody ran OPTIMIZE.
Module Roadmap

Lessons 2–3 cover MERGE – the upsert operation that replaces full overwrite with incremental updates. Lesson 4 covers schema evolution – handling upstream changes without breaking downstream consumers. Lessons 5–6 cover table maintenance – OPTIMIZE, VACUUM, and small file compaction. Lesson 7 covers time travel – using Delta’s version history for debugging and recovery. Lessons 8–9 cover the ripple effects of Delta operations on the SQL analytics endpoint and the Power BI semantic model. Lesson 10 synthesizes everything into NordGrid’s production Delta operations playbook. Every lesson builds on the performance skills from Module 1 – reading plans, checking the Spark UI, and measuring before and after.

blocks.title: Delta maintenance is data engineering, not housekeeping
blocks.content: It is tempting to treat OPTIMIZE and VACUUM as background tasks that someone else handles. In NordGrid’s structure, the data engineer who owns the table owns its maintenance. A Silver table that degrades to 3-second queries because of file fragmentation is the owning engineer’s problem, not a platform team problem. This module treats maintenance as a first-class engineering responsibility – on par with transformation logic and pipeline design.

At junior level, NordGrid’s Silver notebook used mode('overwrite') – every night it rewrote the entire Silver table from all Bronze files. At 500K rows, this took 8 minutes. At 1.3 billion rows, it takes 45 minutes and rewrites 3.2 TB of data that has not changed. MERGE solves this by combining insert and update in a single atomic operation: new rows are inserted, existing rows are updated, and unchanged rows are left untouched. The nightly write drops from 3.2 TB to approximately 3 GB – just today’s new and changed readings – and the runtime drops from 45 minutes to under 5 minutes.
The MERGE Syntax

from delta.tables import DeltaTable

# Load the existing Silver table as a DeltaTable object
silver_table = DeltaTable.forName(spark, "silver_meters")

# Today's cleaned data – the 'source' DataFrame
df_today = (
    spark.read.csv(input_path, header=True, schema=meter_schema)
    .filter(col("meter_id").isNotNull())
    .filter(col("daily_kw") > 0)
    .dropDuplicates(["meter_id", "date"])
    .withColumn("year", year(col("date")))
    .withColumn("month", month(col("date")))
    .withColumn("processing_timestamp", current_timestamp())
)

# MERGE: match on (meter_id, date) – the business key
silver_table.alias("target").merge(
    df_today.alias("source"),
    "target.meter_id = source.meter_id AND target.date = source.date"
).whenMatchedUpdateAll() \
 .whenNotMatchedInsertAll() \
 .execute()

The MERGE operation has three parts. The match condition (target.meter_id = source.meter_id AND target.date = source.date) defines how to find corresponding rows between the target table and the source DataFrame. whenMatchedUpdateAll() says: for rows that exist in both target and source, update the target row with the source values. whenNotMatchedInsertAll() says: for rows in the source that have no match in the target, insert them as new rows. The operation is atomic – either all changes commit or none do. If the notebook fails mid-MERGE, the target table is unchanged.
What Happens Internally

Under the hood, MERGE performs a join between the target and source on the match condition, then classifies each row as matched (update) or not-matched (insert). Spark writes new Parquet files containing the updated and inserted rows, and writes a new transaction log entry that records which old files are replaced and which new files are added. The old files are not deleted immediately – they remain on disk until VACUUM removes them, enabling time travel to the pre-MERGE version. This means that after a MERGE, the table temporarily contains both old and new file versions, consuming extra storage until VACUUM runs.
Selective Updates

# MERGE with selective update – only update if the source value is different
# This avoids rewriting files where nothing actually changed
silver_table.alias("target").merge(
    df_today.alias("source"),
    "target.meter_id = source.meter_id AND target.date = source.date"
).whenMatchedUpdate(
    condition="target.daily_kw != source.daily_kw OR target.region != source.region",
    set={
        "daily_kw": col("source.daily_kw"),
        "region": col("source.region"),
        "processing_timestamp": current_timestamp()
    }
).whenNotMatchedInsertAll() \
 .execute()

The conditional update in the whenMatchedUpdate clause is a production optimization. Without it, MERGE rewrites every matched row even when the values are identical – generating unnecessary file I/O. With the condition, MERGE only rewrites rows where the daily_kw or region actually changed. For NordGrid’s daily meter data, approximately 99.5% of readings are identical to the previous day’s export (the overlapping export window). The conditional update reduces the number of rewritten files from thousands to dozens, cutting MERGE runtime by 80% and reducing the temporary file bloat from MERGE’s copy-on-write behavior.

blocks.title: Match condition determines shuffle cost
blocks.content: The MERGE match condition triggers a shuffle join between the target and source. If the target table has 1.3 billion rows and the source has 1.2 million rows, the join shuffles both sides by the match key (meter_id, date). Partition pruning applies here: if the source only contains March 2025 data and the target is partitioned by year and month, add a partition filter to the match condition or pre-filter the target. NordGrid’s MERGE includes an explicit year/month filter in the merge condition to limit the target scan to the current month’s partition. Without this filter, MERGE scans the entire 3.2 TB table.

MERGE is powerful but expensive when used carelessly. NordGrid’s first MERGE implementation took 38 minutes – longer than the 45-minute overwrite it was supposed to replace. The cause: MERGE scanned the entire 3.2 TB target table to join against 1.2 million source rows because the match condition did not include a partition filter. The join shuffled 3.2 TB of target data plus 3 GB of source data, produced a 3.2 TB intermediate dataset, and wrote back 3 GB of changes. The scan and shuffle were entirely wasteful – only one month’s partition contained matches. This lesson covers the performance characteristics of MERGE and the pitfalls that turn a fast operation into a slow one.
Pitfall 1: Missing Partition Filter

# BAD: MERGE scans entire target table (3.2 TB)
silver_table.alias("target").merge(
    df_today.alias("source"),
    "target.meter_id = source.meter_id AND target.date = source.date"
).whenMatchedUpdateAll() \
 .whenNotMatchedInsertAll() \
 .execute()
# Duration: 38 minutes – target scan reads ALL partitions

# GOOD: Add partition filter to restrict target scan
silver_table.alias("target").merge(
    df_today.alias("source"),
    """target.meter_id = source.meter_id
       AND target.date = source.date
       AND target.year = 2025
       AND target.month = 3"""
).whenMatchedUpdateAll() \
 .whenNotMatchedInsertAll() \
 .execute()
# Duration: 4 minutes – target scan reads only the March 2025 partition

The partition filter in the match condition activates Delta’s partition pruning during the target scan phase. Without it, MERGE reads every partition of the target table to find potential matches – even partitions from 2022 that could not possibly contain a match for March 2025 source data. With the filter, MERGE reads only the year=2025/month=3 partition (approximately 90 GB instead of 3.2 TB). This single change reduced NordGrid’s MERGE from 38 minutes to 4 minutes. The lesson from Module 1 applies directly: always check PartitionFilters in the execution plan, even for MERGE operations.
Pitfall 2: Source DataFrame Too Large

MERGE joins the source DataFrame against the target table. If the source is the entire day’s raw data (1.2M rows) rather than a pre-filtered and deduplicated subset, the join is larger than necessary. NordGrid applies all Silver transformation logic – filtering, deduplication, null handling – before the MERGE, not after. The source DataFrame entering MERGE should be the final, clean dataset. Applying transformations inside or after the MERGE wastes join resources on rows that will be discarded. Pre-MERGE filtering reduced NordGrid’s source from 1.2M to 1.17M rows, which is modest – but for NordGrid’s SCADA table (50M raw rows, 5M after deduplication), pre-filtering reduced MERGE time by 70%.
Pitfall 3: Non-Idempotent MERGE

An overwrite is naturally idempotent – running it twice produces the same result. MERGE with whenNotMatchedInsertAll is not idempotent if the source contains rows that already exist in the target and the match condition fails to catch them. NordGrid’s incident: a pipeline retry caused the MERGE to run twice. The first run inserted 50,000 new rows correctly. The second run should have matched those rows and updated them (a no-op since the values were identical). Instead, the match condition used only meter_id without date, causing rows from different dates to match incorrectly. Some rows were updated with the wrong date’s data, and some new rows were re-inserted as duplicates. The fix was to include all business key columns in the match condition.

left.label: Overwrite (Junior Pattern)
points: Rewrites entire table every run – 3.2 TB at NordGrid scale
Naturally idempotent – running twice produces the same table
Simple: one write call, no join logic
Runtime grows linearly with total table size
NordGrid use: acceptable up to ~500GB total table size

right.label: MERGE (Mid-Level Pattern)
points: Writes only changed/new data – 3 GB at NordGrid scale
Idempotent only if match condition covers the full business key
Complex: join + classify + selective write, needs partition filter
Runtime grows with the number of changed rows, not table size
NordGrid use: required above ~500GB when overwrite exceeds the SLA

blocks.title: MERGE is not always better than overwrite
blocks.content: MERGE is faster than overwrite when the change set is small relative to the total table. But MERGE has higher per-row overhead (join, classify, selective write) than overwrite (bulk write). For a small table (under 100M rows) with a high change rate (most rows updated daily), a full overwrite is simpler, faster, and easier to debug. NordGrid’s region reference table (200 rows) still uses overwrite because MERGE overhead exceeds the savings. The decision rule: use MERGE when the daily change volume is less than 10% of the total table and the total table is large enough that a full overwrite exceeds the SLA.

Schemas change. NordGrid’s meter vendor added a firmware_version column in Q2 2025. The billing team requested a tariff_code column in Q3. The regulatory team changed daily_kw precision from double to decimal(18,4) for audit compliance. Each change rippled through the pipeline: the Bronze files arrived with new columns, the Silver transformation needed updating, the Gold aggregations needed verifying, and the SQL views and semantic models needed refreshing. Schema evolution is not an exceptional event – it is a quarterly routine at NordGrid’s scale, and handling it without downtime requires a deliberate strategy.
Three Categories of Schema Change

rows: New column added to the source
mergeSchema: true – adds the new column, fills existing rows with null
Column type changes to a wider type
mergeSchema may accept (int→long), overwriteSchema for others
Column removed, renamed, or type narrowed
overwriteSchema required – downstream views and models must be updated first
meter_id renamed to device_id (never happened, but planned for)

blocks.content: Additive Changes with mergeSchema

# Additive schema change: source now has firmware_version column
# The Silver table does not have this column yet

# Without mergeSchema – the write FAILS with schema mismatch
# df_today.write.mode("overwrite").saveAsTable("silver_meters")
# ERROR: AnalysisException: incompatible schema

# With mergeSchema – the new column is added to the table
df_today.write \
    .mode("overwrite") \
    .option("mergeSchema", "true") \
    .partitionBy("year", "month") \
    .saveAsTable("silver_meters")

# Result: silver_meters now has firmware_version column
# Existing rows (before this write) have null for firmware_version
# New rows have the actual firmware_version value

The mergeSchema option is additive – it can add columns but cannot remove or rename them. Existing data in the table is not rewritten: old Parquet files retain their original schema (without firmware_version), and Delta’s schema merge logic fills the missing column with null when those files are read. This means the mergeSchema write is fast – it only writes new data files and updates the transaction log’s schema metadata. NordGrid uses mergeSchema for all additive changes because it requires no coordination with downstream consumers: the new column appears as null in historical data and with real values in new data, which is the expected behavior for all consumer queries.
Breaking Changes with overwriteSchema

Breaking changes – column removals, renames, or type narrowing – require overwriteSchema, which replaces the table’s entire schema with the new one. This is a destructive operation: it rewrites the table and any column that existed in the old schema but not in the new one is permanently lost (unless you restore from a time travel version). NordGrid treats breaking changes as deployment events that require coordination: the data engineering team notifies all downstream consumers, verifies that SQL views and semantic models have been updated, runs integration tests in the staging workspace, and only then deploys the breaking change to production during a scheduled maintenance window.
NordGrid’s Schema Change Workflow

items: Classify the change: additive (new column), type-widening (precision change), or breaking (rename/remove). Classification determines the Delta option and the coordination level required.

For additive changes: add the column to the notebook’s StructType schema definition, update the transformation logic if the new column needs processing, and deploy with mergeSchema. No downstream coordination needed – the column appears as null in historical data.

For type-widening changes: test in the staging workspace with mergeSchema. Verify that the SQL endpoint and semantic model reflect the new type correctly. Deploy to production with mergeSchema if Delta accepts the type change, or with overwriteSchema if not.

For breaking changes: notify all downstream consumers (SQL view owners, semantic model owners, report builders). Update views and models in the staging workspace. Run integration tests. Deploy during the scheduled maintenance window with overwriteSchema. Verify all downstream items function correctly post-deploy.

blocks.title: mergeSchema is not overwriteSchema
blocks.content: These two options sound similar but do fundamentally different things. mergeSchema adds new columns to the existing schema – it is additive and safe. overwriteSchema replaces the entire schema – it is destructive and requires coordination. NordGrid’s coding standard prohibits overwriteSchema in any notebook that runs on a schedule. It is allowed only in manually-triggered notebooks with explicit approval from the platform team. The junior-level track’s overwriteSchema: false setting was a safety guard. At mid-level, you understand why that guard exists and when it is appropriate to temporarily remove it.

A Delta table that is written to daily but never maintained degrades over time. Each MERGE adds new files without removing old ones. Each append creates small files that fragment the table’s storage. The transaction log grows with each operation, slowing metadata reads. After six months of NordGrid’s daily pipeline, the Silver table had 4,200 files across 36 partitions – an average of 117 files per partition. Queries that previously returned in 2 seconds took 8 seconds because Spark opened 117 files per partition instead of the optimal 4–8. Table maintenance – OPTIMIZE and VACUUM – is the routine that prevents this degradation.
OPTIMIZE – Compacting Small Files

OPTIMIZE reads all the small files in a table (or partition), merges their data, and writes a smaller number of large files – each approximately 1 GB in size. The operation does not change the table’s logical content: the same rows exist before and after, but they are stored in fewer, larger files. After OPTIMIZE, a partition that had 117 files has 4–6 files, and the query that opened 117 files now opens 4–6 – a 20x reduction in file-open overhead that directly translates to faster scan times.

-- OPTIMIZE the entire Silver table – compacts all partitions
OPTIMIZE silver_meters;

-- OPTIMIZE only the current month's partition – faster, targets the partition
-- that accumulates small files from daily MERGEs
OPTIMIZE silver_meters
WHERE year = 2025 AND month = 3;

-- OPTIMIZE with ZORDER – co-locates data by the specified columns
-- within each file for faster point lookups
OPTIMIZE silver_meters
WHERE year = 2025 AND month = 3
ZORDER BY (region, meter_id);

The ZORDER option in the third example sorts data within each output file by the specified columns. This means that all NORTH region data is physically close together within the file, and a query that filters WHERE region = 'NORTH' can skip the non-NORTH sections of the file using Parquet’s row group statistics. ZORDER provides the benefits of partition pruning for non-partition columns. NordGrid ZORDERs by (region, meter_id) because the most common query patterns filter by region (the operations dashboard) or by meter_id (the billing system’s per-meter lookup).
VACUUM – Removing Obsolete Files

After OPTIMIZE writes new compacted files, the old small files still exist on disk – they are referenced by older versions of the transaction log for time travel purposes. VACUUM removes files that are no longer referenced by any version within the retention period. NordGrid’s retention is 14 days: VACUUM removes files that were superseded more than 14 days ago. Files newer than 14 days are kept to support time travel and debugging. After VACUUM, storage consumption drops because the old small files (which were duplicated by OPTIMIZE’s compacted files) are deleted.

The RETAIN clause specifies the retention period in hours. 336 hours equals 14 days. Delta Lake’s default minimum retention is 168 hours (7 days) – setting it lower triggers a safety error that must be explicitly overridden. NordGrid chose 14 days because it provides a two-week debugging window (enough to investigate most production issues) while preventing storage from growing indefinitely. At NordGrid’s scale, the difference between 7-day and 14-day retention is approximately 100 GB of extra storage – a cost the team considers worthwhile for the extended debugging capability.
The Maintenance Schedule

rows: After the weekly pipeline quiet period
The current month accumulates small files from daily MERGEs; weekly compaction prevents degradation
After the monthly billing pipeline completes
Catches older partitions that were modified by late-arriving data or backfills
OPTIMIZE creates new files; VACUUM removes the old ones that OPTIMIZE replaced

blocks.title: OPTIMIZE before VACUUM, never the reverse
blocks.content: OPTIMIZE creates new compacted files and marks old files as superseded. VACUUM deletes superseded files. If you run VACUUM before OPTIMIZE, there is nothing to clean up – the old small files are still the current version. If you run OPTIMIZE and then forget VACUUM, the table has both old small files and new compacted files, temporarily doubling storage. NordGrid’s maintenance notebook always runs OPTIMIZE first, waits for it to complete, then runs VACUUM. The notebook fails fast if OPTIMIZE encounters an error, preventing VACUUM from running on a partially-optimized table.

OPTIMIZE handles scheduled compaction – a weekly job that merges accumulated small files. But some tables produce small files so aggressively that weekly compaction is not sufficient. NordGrid’s SCADA telemetry table receives data every 10 seconds from 5,000 turbines, written as micro-batch appends. Each append creates one file per partition. After 24 hours, a single day’s partition contains 8,640 files (one per 10-second batch). A query against one day’s SCADA data opens 8,640 files instead of the optimal 4–8. This lesson covers the compaction strategies for high-frequency write scenarios where OPTIMIZE alone is not enough.
Why Small Files Are Expensive

Each file in a Delta table requires a separate I/O operation to open, read the Parquet footer (column statistics, schema), and begin decoding. For large files (500 MB–1 GB), this overhead is negligible compared to the data reading time. For small files (100 KB–10 MB), the overhead dominates – Spark spends more time opening and closing files than reading data. The I/O overhead is proportional to the file count, not the data volume: reading 1 GB from one file takes the same data-reading time as reading 1 GB from 1,000 files, but the 1,000-file version adds 999 extra open/close cycles. At NordGrid’s SCADA scale, the file-open overhead added 45 seconds to a query that should have taken 3 seconds.
Write-Time Compaction

# Enable optimizeWrite – Fabric's write-time file coalescing
# This reduces the number of files created during each write
spark.conf.set("spark.microsoft.delta.optimizeWrite.enabled", "true")

# Combined with V-Order for maximum read performance
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.parquet.vorder.enabled", "true")

# Write with these settings – Fabric automatically coalesces
# small partitions into larger files during the write operation
df_scada.write \
    .mode("append") \
    .partitionBy("date") \
    .saveAsTable("silver_scada")

The optimizeWrite setting tells Fabric’s Spark engine to coalesce small output partitions during the write operation itself – before files are created. Without optimizeWrite, a streaming micro-batch that produces 50 rows per partition writes a 50-row file (a few KB). With optimizeWrite, Spark buffers multiple micro-batches’ output and writes a single larger file. This reduces the file count from 8,640 per day to approximately 30–50 per day, eliminating the need for frequent post-write OPTIMIZE. NordGrid enables optimizeWrite for all streaming and high-frequency append workloads.
Post-Write Compaction Strategies

rows: High-frequency appends – streaming, micro-batch, hourly loads
SCADA telemetry streaming notebook – reduces 8,640 files/day to 30–50
Daily or weekly batch writes that create moderate small files
Silver meter table – weekly OPTIMIZE compacts files from daily MERGEs
Tables that need compaction after every write without manual scheduling
Not used by NordGrid – the predictable schedule is preferred over automatic compaction for production auditability
One-time backfills or full table rewrites where file count control matters
Historical data migration – repartition(48) before saveAsTable ensures 48 appropriately-sized files

blocks.title: Prevent small files at write time, compact what gets through
blocks.content: The best compaction strategy is preventing small files from being created in the first place. optimizeWrite handles this for streaming and micro-batch workloads. Appropriate repartition() handles this for batch writes. OPTIMIZE handles the residual small files that accumulate despite write-time prevention. NordGrid’s approach is layered: optimizeWrite reduces file creation by 95%, and weekly OPTIMIZE catches the remaining 5%. This is more efficient than creating thousands of small files and then compacting them all – prevention is cheaper than cleanup.

sections.title: Time Travel for Debugging and Recovery

blocks.content: At junior level, time travel was a concept – Delta keeps previous versions of the table, and you can read them. At mid-level, time travel becomes a critical operational tool. NordGrid’s production incident: the Silver transformation notebook deployed a change that incorrectly filtered out meters with customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL-NEW' – a new customer classification the filter logic did not account for. The notebook ran nightly for three days before an analyst noticed that residential meter counts were declining. Time travel enabled the team to identify exactly when the data loss started (version 487, Monday night), quantify the impact (23,000 meters missing per day), and restore the table to its pre-incident state while the fix was deployed.
Reading Historical Versions

# Read the Silver table at a specific version (before the bad deployment)
df_before = spark.read.format("delta") \
    .option("versionAsOf", 486) \
    .table("silver_meters")

# Read the table as it was at a specific timestamp
df_at_time = spark.read.format("delta") \
    .option("timestampAsOf", "2025-03-17 01:00:00") \
    .table("silver_meters")

# Compare row counts between the good version and the current version
current_count = spark.read.table("silver_meters") \
    .filter(col("customer_type") == "RESIDENTIAL-NEW") \
    .count()

historical_count = df_before \
    .filter(col("customer_type") == "RESIDENTIAL-NEW") \
    .count()

print(f"Current RESIDENTIAL-NEW: {current_count}")
print(f"Pre-incident RESIDENTIAL-NEW: {historical_count}")
print(f"Missing meters: {historical_count - current_count}")
# Output: Missing meters: 69,000 (23,000/day × 3 days)

The diagnostic code above is the first step in NordGrid’s incident response: quantify the impact. By comparing the current table to the pre-incident version, the team determined that 69,000 meters were missing (23,000 per day for three days). This information drove the priority of the fix – 69,000 missing meters affects billing and regulatory reporting, making it a severity-1 incident requiring immediate remediation. Without time travel, the team would have needed to reprocess three days of Bronze files to quantify the impact, adding hours to the incident response.
Restoring a Previous Version

from delta.tables import DeltaTable

# RESTORE the table to the pre-incident version
# This creates a new version (version 491) that matches version 486's content
dt = DeltaTable.forName(spark, "silver_meters")
dt.restoreToVersion(486)

# Verify the restoration
restored_count = spark.read.table("silver_meters") \
    .filter(col("customer_type") == "RESIDENTIAL-NEW") \
    .count()
print(f"Restored RESIDENTIAL-NEW: {restored_count}")
# Output: Restored RESIDENTIAL-NEW: 115,000 (all meters present)

RESTORE creates a new table version that matches the specified historical version’s content. It does not delete the intermediate versions (487–490) – those remain available for time travel and audit purposes. After RESTORE, the table is in a known-good state while the team fixes the notebook bug. Once the fix is deployed, the pipeline reruns for the three affected days, writing versions 492–494 with the correct filter logic. The RESTORE buys the team time to fix the root cause without leaving the production table in a broken state.
The Version History

-- View the table's version history – shows every write, MERGE, OPTIMIZE, and RESTORE
DESCRIBE HISTORY silver_meters;

-- Output columns: version, timestamp, operation, operationParameters, ...
-- version 491: RESTORE (restoreVersion=486)
-- version 490: MERGE (predicate=...)
-- version 489: MERGE (predicate=...) – bad deployment
-- version 488: MERGE (predicate=...) – bad deployment
-- version 487: MERGE (predicate=...) – first bad version
-- version 486: MERGE (predicate=...) – last good version

blocks.title: Time travel is bounded by VACUUM retention
blocks.content: Time travel only works for versions whose files still exist on disk. If NordGrid’s VACUUM retention is 14 days, versions older than 14 days have their files deleted – attempting to read them fails with a FileNotFoundException. This means the debugging window is 14 days. NordGrid’s retention policy is a deliberate trade-off: 14 days provides enough time to catch and investigate most production issues, while preventing storage from growing indefinitely. For high-criticality tables, NordGrid’s platform team is considering extending retention to 30 days – but the storage cost doubles.

sections.title: Delta Lake and the SQL Endpoint

blocks.content: Every Delta table change – MERGE, OPTIMIZE, VACUUM, schema evolution – ripples outward to the SQL analytics endpoint that analysts query every day. The endpoint automatically reflects Delta table changes, but the timing, the performance impact, and the edge cases are not always obvious. NordGrid’s analysts experienced three SQL-related problems after the team adopted mid-level Delta operations: queries returned stale results for several seconds after a MERGE, OPTIMIZE temporarily doubled query time before the endpoint’s statistics refreshed, and a mergeSchema change caused a SQL view to break because the view referenced a column that was renamed. This lesson covers how Delta operations affect the SQL endpoint and how to manage the interaction.
MERGE and SQL Visibility

When a MERGE completes in a notebook, the transaction log records the new version. The SQL analytics endpoint detects the new version within seconds – typically 5–15 seconds, depending on endpoint load. During this propagation window, SQL queries may return the pre-MERGE data. For NordGrid’s daily pipeline (MERGE completes at 02:05 UTC, analysts query at 08:00 UTC), the propagation delay is invisible. But for the SCADA team’s near-real-time queries (MERGE runs every 15 minutes, analysts query continuously), the 5–15 second delay matters. The team accounts for this by documenting the expected staleness and not treating sub-minute freshness as an SLA.
OPTIMIZE and Query Performance

OPTIMIZE creates new compacted files and marks old files as superseded. The SQL endpoint’s query planner caches file metadata and statistics. After OPTIMIZE, the cache contains stale metadata pointing to old files. The endpoint refreshes its cache automatically, but the refresh can take 30–60 seconds. During this window, SQL queries may run slower (they read old statistics and choose suboptimal plans) or slightly faster (they benefit from the compacted files but with stale statistics). NordGrid schedules OPTIMIZE to complete at 04:30 UTC – two hours before the earliest analyst activity – so the cache refresh completes before anyone queries the table.
Schema Changes and SQL Views

-- Before schema change: view references daily_kw
CREATE OR ALTER VIEW v_monthly_region_summary AS
SELECT region, YEAR(date) AS year, MONTH(date) AS month,
       ROUND(SUM(daily_kw), 2) AS total_kw
FROM silver_meters
GROUP BY region, YEAR(date), MONTH(date);

-- After mergeSchema adds firmware_version column:
-- The view still works – it does not reference the new column
-- The new column appears in the table schema but the view ignores it

-- If the schema change RENAMED daily_kw to daily_kwh:
-- The view BREAKS with: Invalid column name 'daily_kw'
-- Fix: update the view before or immediately after the schema change
CREATE OR ALTER VIEW v_monthly_region_summary AS
SELECT region, YEAR(date) AS year, MONTH(date) AS month,
       ROUND(SUM(daily_kwh), 2) AS total_kw  -- Updated column reference
FROM silver_meters
GROUP BY region, YEAR(date), MONTH(date);

Additive schema changes (new columns) do not break existing views – the view simply does not see the new column unless it is added to the view’s SELECT list. Breaking schema changes (renames, removals) break any view that references the changed column. NordGrid’s schema change workflow (lesson 4) includes a mandatory step: before deploying a breaking change, identify all SQL views that reference the affected column using the SQL endpoint’s metadata queries, and update those views as part of the deployment. The deployment notebook updates views and tables in the same transaction window.

blocks.title: Schedule OPTIMIZE before the analyst window
blocks.content: The SQL analytics endpoint performs best when it reads from compacted, well-organized Delta files. Schedule OPTIMIZE to complete at least 30 minutes before the first analyst query of the day so that the endpoint’s metadata cache refreshes completely. NordGrid’s maintenance window (04:00–05:00 UTC) is chosen specifically because analysts start querying at 07:30 UTC – a 2.5-hour buffer that absorbs any OPTIMIZE delay or cache refresh latency.

sections.title: Delta Lake and the Semantic Model

blocks.content: The SQL analytics endpoint is one consumer of Delta table changes. The Power BI semantic model is another. When a Delta table’s data or schema changes, the semantic model’s Direct Lake connection reflects those changes – but the interaction is more nuanced than the SQL endpoint’s because the semantic model caches column metadata, data type mappings, and relationship definitions. A schema change that the SQL endpoint absorbs seamlessly may require manual intervention in the semantic model. This lesson covers how Delta operations affect the semantic model and the coordination patterns that prevent broken reports.
Direct Lake and Delta Versions

Direct Lake reads from the latest version of the Delta table’s Parquet files. When a MERGE writes a new version, Direct Lake sees the new data on the next report query – no scheduled refresh needed. This is one of Direct Lake’s key advantages over Import mode. However, Direct Lake caches the table’s schema and column metadata. If a mergeSchema change adds a new column, the semantic model does not automatically include that column – the model’s metadata cache must be refreshed. NordGrid’s data engineers trigger a semantic model refresh after any schema change by calling the Fabric REST API from the deployment notebook.
Schema Changes and the Semantic Model

rows: Column NOT visible until model refresh
Trigger semantic model refresh from the deployment notebook
New type visible after cache refresh
May need model metadata update if the type change affects measures
Refresh model; verify any DAX measures that reference the column
Views that reference the column break
Report visuals that reference the column break; relationships may break
Update model before deploying the table change – requires coordinated deployment
No schema impact – data is recompacted
No impact – Direct Lake reads the compacted files seamlessly
None – OPTIMIZE is transparent to the semantic model
No impact – only obsolete files removed
No impact – Direct Lake only reads current version files
None – VACUUM is transparent to the semantic model

blocks.content: The Coordinated Deployment Pattern

Breaking schema changes require simultaneous updates to the Delta table, the SQL views, and the semantic model. If the table is updated first, the semantic model shows errors until it is refreshed. If the model is updated first (adding the new column reference), it fails because the column does not yet exist in the table. NordGrid’s deployment sequence for breaking changes: (1) deploy the notebook and pipeline changes to production but do not run the pipeline yet, (2) update the SQL views with the new column references, (3) update the semantic model with the new column mappings, (4) trigger the pipeline to write the new schema, (5) verify all three layers reflect the change correctly.

For additive changes, the sequence is simpler because nothing breaks: (1) deploy and run the notebook with mergeSchema, (2) trigger the semantic model refresh so the new column appears, (3) optionally update SQL views to include the new column if analysts need it. The existing reports and views continue to work throughout because they do not reference the new column. NordGrid’s automation notebook handles step 2 automatically – it calls the Fabric REST API to trigger a semantic model refresh after every schema-changing pipeline run.

blocks.title: Schema changes are cross-layer events
blocks.content: In a traditional data warehouse, a schema change affects one layer – the database table. In Fabric, a schema change to a Delta table ripples through three layers: the Lakehouse table, the SQL analytics endpoint, and the semantic model. Mid-level data engineers must think about all three layers when planning a schema change. The deployment notebook, the SQL view updates, and the semantic model refresh are not separate tasks – they are one coordinated deployment that spans the entire data stack.

The previous nine lessons covered MERGE for incremental writes, MERGE performance tuning with partition filters and selective updates, schema evolution handling across additive, type-widening, and breaking changes, OPTIMIZE and VACUUM for scheduled table maintenance, small file compaction strategies for high-frequency writes, time travel for production debugging and incident recovery, and the interaction between Delta operations and both the SQL analytics endpoint and the Power BI semantic model. This lesson synthesizes everything into NordGrid’s production Delta operations playbook – the document that every data engineer on the team follows when operating Delta tables in Fabric.
The Delta Table Lifecycle

blocks.title: NordGrid’s Delta table lifecycle: write, maintain, evolve, recover

blocks.caption: The four operational modes of a production Delta table: daily writes (MERGE), scheduled maintenance (OPTIMIZE/VACUUM), occasional schema evolution, and rare recovery from incidents. Each mode has its own workflow, frequency, and coordination requirements.

blocks.content: The Monitoring Dashboard

rows: Table size vs row count ratio
> 10 minutes – check partition filter, source size

blocks.content: What Comes Next

Module 3 takes the pipeline from Module 7 of the junior track (a linear Copy–Notebook sequence) and adds the production patterns that mid-level scale requires: error handling with retry logic, conditional branching with If activities, ForEach loops for per-region processing, parent-child pipeline orchestration, and backfill patterns for reprocessing historical data. The MERGE and maintenance operations from this module become activities in those advanced pipelines – the weekly OPTIMIZE is a scheduled pipeline activity, the schema change deployment is a coordinated multi-activity pipeline, and the incident recovery RESTORE is a manually-triggered pipeline with time travel parameters.

blocks.title: A well-maintained Delta table is a fast Delta table
blocks.content: Every performance technique from Module 1 – partition pruning, column pruning, optimized scan times – depends on a well-maintained Delta table. A fragmented table with 4,000 small files defeats partition pruning because Spark must open every file. A table with stale statistics defeats the query optimizer because it makes wrong cardinality estimates. OPTIMIZE and VACUUM are not administrative chores – they are performance prerequisites. NordGrid’s weekly maintenance reduces the Silver table’s average query time by 35% compared to the pre-maintenance baseline. That 35% is the return on investment for 15 minutes of weekly maintenance compute.

chapters.title: Advanced Pipeline Patterns
chapters.description: Build production-grade Data Factory pipelines with error handling, retry logic, conditional branching, ForEach loops, and parameterized multi-environment deployment.

blocks.content: In the junior-level track, NordGrid’s pipeline was a straight line: Copy activity downloads the CSV, Notebook activity transforms it, success notification fires. That linear pipeline served 4 regions and 500K meters without a problem. At mid-level scale – 12 regions, 1.2M meters, 3 consumer teams – the linear pipeline is insufficient. The SFTP server is unreliable and needs retry logic. Some regions fail while others succeed, and a single failure should not halt the entire pipeline. The operations team wants per-region status notifications, not just a blanket success or failure message. And the pipeline must support both daily scheduled runs and ad-hoc backfills with different date ranges. This module teaches the pipeline patterns that handle these production realities.
What Linear Pipelines Cannot Do

rows: Pipeline fails immediately, on-call engineer retries manually
Retry logic with exponential backoff retries automatically
One region’s data is malformed, others are fine
Entire pipeline fails – no region gets processed
ForEach loop with per-region error isolation – 11 regions succeed, 1 fails and is reported
Bronze file is empty (vendor export error)
Notebook runs on empty data, writes empty Silver table
If Condition checks file row count, skips transformation when zero
Need to reprocess last 30 days after a schema fix
Manual pipeline edits to change dates, run 30 times
Parameterized backfill pipeline with date range loop
Pipeline complexity grows with new sources
One giant pipeline becomes unreadable and untestable
Parent-child orchestration – master pipeline calls sub-pipelines

blocks.content: The Module Roadmap

Lessons 2–3 cover resilience: error handling with retry logic and conditional branching with If activities. These patterns prevent unnecessary failures and enable graceful degradation when individual components break. Lessons 4–5 cover scale: ForEach loops for per-region processing and dynamic configuration from Lakehouse tables. These patterns let a single pipeline handle 12 regions without 12 copies of the same logic. Lessons 6–7 cover architecture: parent-child orchestration and backfill patterns. These patterns manage complexity as the pipeline grows from 3 activities to 30. Lessons 8–9 cover operations: monitoring with custom metrics and testing strategies for pipelines. Lesson 10 synthesizes everything into NordGrid’s complete production pipeline architecture.
The Complexity Budget

Every advanced pattern in this module adds complexity. ForEach loops add iteration logic. If conditions add branching logic. Parent-child pipelines add coordination overhead. The goal is not to use every pattern in every pipeline – it is to use the minimum set of patterns that meets the production requirements. NordGrid’s daily meter pipeline uses ForEach (for 12 regions), If (for empty file detection), and retry (for SFTP reliability). It does not use parent-child orchestration because the pipeline has only 5 activities and does not benefit from decomposition. The SCADA pipeline, with 15 activities across 3 data sources, does use parent-child. Match the pattern to the complexity, not the other way around.

blocks.title: Simplicity is a feature, not a limitation
blocks.content: The junior-level linear pipeline was not wrong – it was appropriately simple for its scale. A linear pipeline with 3 activities is easier to debug, monitor, and explain than a pipeline with ForEach loops, conditional branches, and parent-child calls. Add complexity only when the production requirements demand it. If a linear pipeline meets the SLA and handles failures acceptably, leave it linear. NordGrid’s reference table pipeline is still linear – it copies, transforms, and notifies. No ForEach, no branching. Simplicity serves that use case perfectly.

NordGrid’s SFTP server goes unreachable approximately twice per month – typically for 2–5 minutes during the vendor’s maintenance window, which occasionally drifts into NordGrid’s 02:00 UTC ingestion time. At junior level, this caused a pipeline failure, a Slack alert, and a manual retry by the on-call engineer at 07:00 when they saw the alert. At mid-level, the pipeline handles this automatically: the Copy activity retries twice with a 5-minute interval, and the SFTP server is back online by the second retry. The on-call engineer sleeps through the night. This lesson covers the retry configuration, the distinction between transient and permanent failures, and the dead-letter pattern for isolating records that fail repeatedly.
Activity-Level Retry Configuration

Every activity on the Fabric pipeline canvas has retry settings in its properties panel: retry count (how many times to retry after failure), retry interval (seconds between retries), and timeout (maximum time before the activity is declared failed). The retry count determines how many additional attempts the activity makes after the initial failure – a retry count of 2 means the activity runs up to 3 times total (1 initial + 2 retries). NordGrid’s Copy activity uses retry count 2 and retry interval 300 seconds (5 minutes). If the SFTP server is unreachable at 02:00, the Copy retries at 02:05 and 02:10. Two retries with 5-minute intervals cover NordGrid’s typical 2–5 minute SFTP outage window.
Transient vs Permanent Failures

left.label: Transient Failure (retry will help)
points: Network timeout – SFTP server temporarily unreachable
Spark session startup timeout – capacity pool temporarily full
Throttling – Fabric capacity temporarily exhausted
Appropriate response: retry with backoff

right.label: Permanent Failure (retry will not help)
points: File not found – the vendor did not generate today’s export
Schema mismatch – the Bronze file has different columns than expected
Authentication failure – the connection credential has expired
Appropriate response: fail fast, alert the team, do not retry

blocks.content: The distinction matters because retrying a permanent failure wastes time and can mask the real problem. If the vendor did not generate today’s file, retrying 3 times with 5-minute intervals adds 15 minutes of delay before the pipeline reports failure. NordGrid’s approach: set retries for activities that commonly experience transient failures (Copy activities accessing external systems) and set retries to 0 for activities where failure is always permanent (Notebook activities where a code error will not resolve itself on retry). The exception is Notebook activities that might fail due to Spark session timeouts – those get 1 retry because the second attempt may get a session from the warm pool.
Retry with Exponential Backoff

Fabric’s retry interval is fixed – you specify a constant number of seconds between retries. True exponential backoff (1 second, then 4, then 16, then 64) is not natively supported. NordGrid works around this by setting a moderate fixed interval (300 seconds) that covers most transient failures. For activities that need more sophisticated retry logic – like an API call that should back off exponentially – NordGrid wraps the call in a Web activity inside an Until loop with a variable that doubles the wait time after each iteration. This is more complex but necessary for rate-limited external APIs that return HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests).
The Failure Notification Pattern

When all retries are exhausted and the activity still fails, the pipeline must notify the team. NordGrid uses a Web activity connected to the failing activity’s red (failure) dependency arrow. The Web activity sends a POST request to a Slack webhook with a JSON payload containing the pipeline name, the failed activity name, the error message, and the processing date. The Slack notification is the on-call engineer’s trigger to investigate. Without this notification, the failure would go unnoticed until someone checks the monitoring hub – which might not happen until the morning standup, by which time the operations dashboard shows 6 hours of stale data.

blocks.title: Retry count × retry interval defines the maximum delay
blocks.content: A retry count of 3 with a 600-second interval means the pipeline waits up to 30 minutes (3 × 600 seconds) before declaring final failure. During those 30 minutes, downstream activities are blocked. NordGrid caps the maximum retry delay at 15 minutes (retry count 2 × interval 300 seconds) for all scheduled pipeline activities. If the failure is not resolved in 15 minutes, it is unlikely to be a transient issue, and the delay is better spent on human investigation than on automated retries that will fail.

NordGrid’s junior-level pipeline ran the Silver transformation notebook regardless of what the Copy activity delivered. If the Bronze file was empty (a vendor export error that happens approximately once per quarter), the notebook ran against zero rows, the Silver table was overwritten with an empty DataFrame, and the operations dashboard showed zero consumption for every region. The analyst team filed a severity-1 incident. The root cause was not a transformation bug – it was a pipeline that did not check its inputs. The If Condition activity prevents this by evaluating an expression at runtime and branching the pipeline into a True path (proceed normally) or a False path (skip the transformation, send an alert).
The If Condition Activity

The If Condition activity evaluates a boolean expression and branches execution into two paths. Drag the activity from the palette onto the canvas, configure the expression in the properties panel, and define activities for both the True and False branches. Each branch is a nested canvas – you can place any activities inside it, including other If conditions, ForEach loops, or Notebook activities. The expression uses Fabric’s pipeline expression language (the same @ syntax from the junior-level track’s lesson on parameters and dynamic content), and it must resolve to a boolean value at runtime.
NordGrid’s Empty File Check

NordGrid’s implementation uses a Lookup activity before the If condition. The Lookup activity reads the first row of the Bronze file and returns its row count. The If condition evaluates whether the count is greater than zero. If True, the pipeline proceeds to the Silver transformation. If False, the pipeline skips the transformation and sends a notification: ‘Bronze file for {processing_date} is empty – vendor export may have failed. Silver table NOT updated.’ This notification is critical – it tells the operations team that the stale dashboard data is intentional (the pipeline detected the problem) rather than accidental (the pipeline broke silently).
Other Branching Patterns

rows: Silver meter pipeline after schema changes

blocks.title: Always define both branches
blocks.content: When you create an If Condition activity, both the True and False branches are optional – you can leave one empty. Resist this temptation. An empty False branch means the pipeline silently skips processing when the condition is false, with no notification and no audit trail. NordGrid’s convention requires every If Condition to have at least a notification activity in the unused branch. If the True branch runs the transformation, the False branch sends an alert explaining why the transformation was skipped. This turns silent skips into documented decisions.

NordGrid now has 12 regions. The Silver transformation notebook is parameterized by region – it can process any single region’s data. At junior level with 4 regions, NordGrid ran one notebook that processed all regions together. At 12 regions, processing all regions in a single notebook creates two problems: if one region’s data is malformed, the entire transformation fails (losing 11 good regions because of 1 bad one), and the single notebook cannot parallelize across regions (processing 12 regions sequentially takes 12× longer than processing them in parallel). The ForEach activity solves both problems by iterating over a collection of items and executing the same activities for each item, either sequentially or in parallel.
How ForEach Works

The ForEach activity takes a JSON array as input and iterates over its elements. For each element, it executes the activities defined inside its body – a nested canvas similar to the If Condition’s branches. The current iteration’s element is accessible via @item() inside the body. NordGrid’s ForEach iterates over the array ['NORTH-1', 'NORTH-2', 'NORTH-3', 'SOUTH-1', ..., 'WEST-3'] – 12 region codes. For each region code, it runs the Silver transformation notebook with the region as a parameter. Each iteration is independent: if NORTH-2’s data is malformed and the notebook fails, the other 11 iterations continue.

The isSequential flag controls parallelism. When false (NordGrid’s setting), the ForEach runs iterations in parallel. The batchCount limits how many iterations run simultaneously – NordGrid sets this to 4 because the F64 capacity can comfortably run 4 concurrent Spark notebooks without throttling. With 12 regions and a batch size of 4, the ForEach runs 3 batches: regions 1–4 in parallel, then 5–8, then 9–12. If each notebook takes 3 minutes, the total ForEach duration is approximately 9 minutes (3 batches × 3 minutes) instead of 36 minutes (12 sequential runs). The 4x speedup comes from parallelism, and the error isolation comes from independent iterations.
Error Isolation

When a ForEach iteration fails, the default behavior is to continue the remaining iterations. After all iterations complete, the ForEach activity itself reports failure if any iteration failed. NordGrid adds a failure notification inside the ForEach body: after the Notebook activity, a Web activity connected with a failure dependency sends a Slack alert with the region code that failed. This means the team receives one alert per failed region (not one alert for the entire pipeline), and the message includes the specific region code and error message. The other 11 regions complete successfully and their data reaches the Silver table on time.
Choosing the Batch Size

The batch size is a capacity decision, not just a parallelism decision. Each concurrent Notebook activity consumes Spark capacity – executor memory, CPU cores, and CU allocation. Running 12 notebooks in parallel on an F64 capacity would starve each notebook of resources, causing all 12 to run slowly instead of 4 running fast. NordGrid’s batch size of 4 was determined empirically: the team tested batch sizes of 2, 4, 8, and 12, measuring total ForEach duration and per-notebook duration. Batch size 4 produced the shortest total duration because each notebook had enough capacity to run at full speed. Batch size 8 was slower overall because per-notebook duration increased from 3 minutes to 6 minutes due to capacity contention.

blocks.title: ForEach failure behavior is configurable
blocks.content: By default, ForEach continues all iterations even when some fail. This is usually the right behavior for data pipelines – process what you can, report what failed. But it means the ForEach activity’s overall status is ‘Failed’ even if 11 of 12 iterations succeeded. Downstream activities connected with a success dependency will not run. NordGrid handles this by connecting the downstream Gold aggregation to the ForEach with a completion dependency (blue arrow) instead of a success dependency (green arrow), and adding a check inside the Gold notebook that verifies how many regions have fresh data before proceeding.

The junior-level pipeline had 3 parameters: processing_date, input_path_base, and output_table. At mid-level, NordGrid’s pipeline needs 15 configuration values: the region list, the SLA threshold per pipeline stage, the Slack webhook URL, the retry count, the batch size, the maintenance window start time, and more. Hardcoding these as pipeline parameters works but creates two problems: changing any value requires editing the pipeline definition, and the same pipeline deployed in dev, staging, and production needs different values for each environment. Dynamic configuration solves both by reading configuration from a Lakehouse table at runtime.
The Configuration Table Pattern

NordGrid stores pipeline configuration in a Delta table called pipeline_config in the Silver Lakehouse. The table has three columns: config_key (string), config_value (string), and environment (string). The pipeline reads its configuration at startup using a Lookup activity that queries the table for the current environment. The environment is the one value that remains a pipeline parameter – it determines which row set to read from the configuration table. The remaining configuration values come from the table, not from the pipeline definition.
Environment-Specific Configuration

The same pipeline runs in all three environments with different behavior driven entirely by the configuration table. In development, it processes 2 regions with no retries and a 60-minute SLA – fast iteration for testing new pipeline logic. In staging, it processes 4 regions with 1 retry and a 30-minute SLA – realistic enough to catch configuration errors and capacity issues before production. In production, it processes all 12 regions with full retry logic and a 20-minute SLA. The pipeline code is identical across environments – only the configuration table values differ.

blocks.title: Configuration in a table, not in the pipeline
blocks.content: Storing configuration in a Lakehouse table rather than in pipeline parameters has three advantages. First, changing configuration does not require editing the pipeline – update the table row and the next pipeline run picks up the new value. Second, configuration changes are auditable – the Delta table’s transaction log records who changed what and when. Third, configuration is version-controlled through Delta’s time travel – if a configuration change causes a problem, the team can restore the previous config version. NordGrid’s platform team manages the production configuration table with the same rigor as production code: changes require a pull request equivalent (a reviewed config update ticket).
