NordGrid’s complete daily workflow has grown from 3 activities (junior level) to approximately 20 activities: Bronze ingestion for meters and SCADA, Silver transformation with per-region ForEach, Gold aggregation, reference table refresh, maintenance operations, and monitoring notifications. Placing all 20 activities on a single pipeline canvas creates a management problem: the canvas is crowded, debugging requires scrolling through unrelated activities, and a change to the SCADA ingestion logic risks accidentally breaking the meter transformation. Parent-child orchestration decomposes the monolithic pipeline into smaller, focused sub-pipelines coordinated by a master pipeline.
The Invoke Pipeline Activity

The Invoke Pipeline activity calls another pipeline as a sub-pipeline, passing parameters and waiting for it to complete. The calling pipeline (parent) pauses until the invoked pipeline (child) finishes, then continues based on the child’s result (success, failure, or completion). Parameters flow from parent to child through the invocation configuration. NordGrid’s master pipeline (pl_master_daily) invokes three child pipelines: pl_bronze_ingest (Copy activities for all sources), pl_silver_transform (ForEach loop with per-region notebooks), and pl_gold_aggregate (Gold notebook and Warehouse refresh). Each child pipeline is independently testable, independently deployable, and independently debuggable.
When to Decompose

Not every pipeline benefits from parent-child decomposition. NordGrid’s rule of thumb: decompose when the pipeline has more than 10 activities, when the pipeline serves multiple logical stages (Bronze, Silver, Gold), or when different team members own different parts of the pipeline. A 5-activity pipeline that one engineer owns should stay monolithic – the overhead of parent-child coordination (parameter passing, error propagation, monitoring across multiple runs) exceeds the benefit. NordGrid’s daily meter pipeline was decomposed at 15 activities. The weekly reference refresh pipeline (5 activities, single owner) remains monolithic.
Error Propagation

When a child pipeline fails, the Invoke Pipeline activity in the parent reports failure. The parent can handle this with the same dependency patterns as any other activity: a success arrow to the next invoke, a failure arrow to an alert. The key subtlety is that the child pipeline’s detailed error information – which specific activity failed, what the error message was – is not automatically available in the parent. NordGrid’s convention requires each child pipeline to write a summary record to a pipeline_run_log table before completing, including the status (success/failure), the failed activity name (if any), and the error message. The parent reads this table after each invoke to include detailed error context in its notifications.

blocks.title: Monitor children independently
blocks.content: The monitoring hub shows each pipeline run separately – the parent and each child appear as independent runs. When diagnosing a failure, the engineer must open the parent run to see which invoke failed, then open the corresponding child run to see which activity inside the child failed. NordGrid’s monitoring dashboard aggregates parent and child run statuses into a single view, but the out-of-the-box monitoring hub requires manual drill-down. Train the on-call team to follow the parent–child trail when investigating failures.

Production pipelines break. Schema fixes, filter corrections, and transformation logic updates all require reprocessing historical data. NordGrid’s mid-level track has already encountered this: Module 2’s schema evolution lesson required reprocessing 30 days of Silver data after a filter bug was discovered. At junior level, reprocessing meant manually editing the pipeline’s processing_date parameter and running it 30 times. At mid-level, reprocessing is automated: a parameterized backfill pipeline accepts a start date and end date, generates the list of dates between them, and processes each date using a ForEach loop. The same pipeline handles both daily scheduled runs and multi-day backfills.
The Dual-Mode Pipeline

NordGrid’s production pipeline supports two modes controlled by a parameter. In daily mode (the default for scheduled runs), the pipeline processes a single date – today’s data. In backfill mode (triggered manually), the pipeline processes a range of dates. The mode parameter determines which date generation logic runs: daily mode sets the date list to a single-element array containing today’s date; backfill mode generates an array of all dates between start_date and end_date. The ForEach activity iterates over this array regardless of its length – one date for daily, thirty dates for backfill. The transformation logic inside the ForEach is identical in both modes.
Idempotency Requirement

Backfill pipelines must be idempotent: processing the same date twice must produce the same result as processing it once. If the Silver notebook uses MERGE (from Module 2), re-merging a date that was already processed updates existing rows and inserts new ones – the result is the same regardless of how many times the MERGE runs. If the Silver notebook uses overwrite mode, re-running overwrites the same partition – also idempotent. The danger zone is append mode: appending the same date twice creates duplicates. NordGrid prohibits append mode in any notebook that might be invoked by a backfill pipeline. All Silver writes use either MERGE or overwrite, both of which are safe for reprocessing.
Backfill Sequencing

The ForEach in backfill mode runs sequentially (isSequential: true) rather than in parallel. This is a deliberate choice: parallel backfill of 30 dates would launch 30 Spark notebooks simultaneously, overwhelming the capacity pool and potentially corrupting the Silver table if multiple notebooks try to MERGE into the same partition concurrently. Sequential processing takes longer (30 dates × 5 minutes = 150 minutes) but is safe and predictable. NordGrid’s backfill policy allows parallel processing only when dates are guaranteed to write to different partitions – for example, backfilling 12 months where each month writes to a distinct year/month partition.

blocks.title: Backfill after the fix, not during development
blocks.content: A common mistake is to start backfilling immediately after discovering a data problem, before the fix is fully tested. NordGrid’s protocol: (1) identify the root cause, (2) deploy the fix to the staging workspace, (3) run the backfill in staging against a sample of dates to verify correctness, (4) deploy the fix to production, (5) then run the production backfill. Backfilling with an untested fix risks reprocessing all 30 days incorrectly – creating a worse problem than the original one.

The junior-level track covered the monitoring hub for checking pipeline run status. At mid-level, monitoring expands from reactive checking (did last night’s pipeline succeed?) to proactive observation (is pipeline performance degrading over time? are durations trending upward? is the SFTP server’s reliability declining?). NordGrid builds this proactive monitoring by writing custom metrics to a monitoring table after every pipeline run, then visualizing those metrics in a dashboard that the team reviews weekly. This lesson covers the metrics to capture, the monitoring table design, and the alerting patterns that turn data into action.
Custom Pipeline Metrics

NordGrid’s pipeline writes a monitoring record to a pipeline_metrics table at the end of every run. The record captures the pipeline name, run ID, processing date, start time, end time, duration in seconds, status (success/failure/partial), the number of regions processed successfully, the number of regions failed, the Silver row count, and the capacity units consumed. The monitoring table accumulates these records over time, creating a time series that reveals trends: is the pipeline getting slower? Are more regions failing? Is the row count stable?
SLA Monitoring

NordGrid’s SLA for the daily meter pipeline is 20 minutes (total end-to-end duration). The pipeline writes its duration to the metrics table after every run, and a separate monitoring pipeline runs at 03:30 UTC to check whether the daily pipeline completed within the SLA. If the duration exceeds 20 minutes, the monitoring pipeline sends a critical alert to the on-call engineer. If the duration is between 15 and 20 minutes (headroom below 25%), the monitoring pipeline sends a warning to the team channel. This two-tier alerting catches both SLA breaches and SLA trends before they become breaches.
Weekly Trend Review

Every Monday, NordGrid’s data engineering team reviews the pipeline_metrics table for the past week. They check: average duration (is it trending upward?), maximum duration (was there a spike?), failure rate (how many regions failed across the week?), row count trend (is the daily volume stable or growing?), and capacity consumption (is the pipeline consuming more CUs than budgeted?). This 15-minute weekly review catches gradual degradation that daily monitoring misses – a pipeline that takes 14 minutes today, 14.5 next month, and 15.2 the month after will breach the 20-minute SLA in 6 months without intervention.

blocks.title: Metrics turn operations into engineering
blocks.content: Without metrics, pipeline operations are reactive: something breaks, someone fixes it. With metrics, operations become engineering: the team sees trends, predicts problems, and intervenes before users are affected. NordGrid’s pipeline_metrics table is the foundation of this shift. The table costs almost nothing (one row per pipeline run, a few KB per day) and provides almost everything: duration tracking, failure analysis, capacity planning, and SLA management. If your pipeline does not write metrics, you are operating blind.

NordGrid’s data engineers test their notebooks extensively (unit tests for transformation functions, integration tests against sample data). But the pipelines that orchestrate those notebooks are deployed untested – the engineer builds the pipeline on the canvas, eyeballs the configuration, and promotes it to production. When a pipeline parameter expression has a typo, the pipeline fails on its first scheduled run at 02:00 UTC, and the on-call engineer discovers it at 07:00. Pipeline testing prevents these avoidable failures by validating the pipeline’s structure, expressions, and end-to-end behavior before it reaches production.
The Dry Run Pattern

A dry run validates the pipeline’s configuration without executing data operations. NordGrid implements dry runs by adding a pipeline parameter called dry_run (boolean, default false). When dry_run is true, Copy activities are skipped (using an If Condition), Notebook activities print their parameters without executing transformations, and the pipeline writes a validation record to the metrics table instead of actual data. The dry run verifies that all expressions resolve correctly, all parameter references are valid, all connection references exist, and all activity dependencies are logically consistent. NordGrid runs a dry run every time a pipeline is modified, before the first real execution.
The Staging Environment

Dry runs validate structure but not behavior. A staging environment validates behavior by running the pipeline against a representative subset of real data in a non-production workspace. NordGrid’s staging workspace (ws_nordgrid_staging) contains a 1% sample of production data – approximately 13,000 meter readings per day across 2 regions. The staging pipeline uses the same pipeline definition as production but reads from the staging Lakehouse (configured through the environment parameter). Running the pipeline in staging reveals problems that dry runs miss: Spark session failures, data type mismatches, MERGE conflicts, and performance regressions.
The Promotion Checklist

items: Dry run passes: all expressions resolve, all connections valid, all dependencies correct. No runtime errors during the dry run execution.

Staging run succeeds: the pipeline completes in the staging workspace with sample data. The Silver table contains the expected rows, null counts are zero for required columns, and region counts match expectations.

Duration within SLA: the staging run duration, extrapolated to production data volume, is within 75% of the SLA. A staging run of 3 minutes on 1% data suggests approximately 5 minutes at full volume – within the 20-minute SLA with comfortable headroom.

Error handling verified: deliberately trigger a failure (provide a nonexistent file path) and verify that the retry logic activates, the failure notification fires, and the pipeline recovers gracefully.
Metrics written: verify that the pipeline_metrics table contains a record from the staging run with correct values for all fields.
Code review approved: a second engineer has reviewed the pipeline canvas, the parameter configuration, the expression syntax, and the ForEach/If Condition logic.

blocks.title: Test the failure paths, not just the happy path
blocks.content: Most pipeline testing verifies that the pipeline succeeds when everything goes right. NordGrid’s checklist includes deliberate failure testing: provide an empty file (does the If Condition skip correctly?), provide a malformed file for one region (does the ForEach isolate the failure?), and disable the SFTP connection (does the retry logic activate and the failure alert fire?). These tests take 10 minutes and prevent the failure paths from being discovered in production at 02:00 UTC for the first time.

The previous nine lessons covered error handling with retry logic for transient failures, conditional branching for input validation with If activities, ForEach loops for per-region parallel processing, dynamic configuration from Lakehouse tables for multi-environment deployment, parent-child orchestration for managing pipeline complexity, backfill patterns for reprocessing historical data idempotently, custom monitoring metrics written to a pipeline_metrics table, and pipeline testing strategies including dry runs and staging environment validation. This lesson assembles everything into NordGrid’s complete production pipeline architecture – the system that runs every night, processes 12 regions, handles failures gracefully, writes monitoring metrics, and meets the 20-minute SLA with 30% headroom.
The Operational Runbook

rows: Check SFTP server status with vendor; retry manually if server is back
If not resolved in 30 min: escalate to vendor support
Check the region’s Bronze file for corruption; reprocess manually
If 3+ regions fail: halt pipeline, investigate systemic issue
Check Spark UI for performance regression (Module 1 workflow)
If not resolved by 08:00: notify operations team of stale data
Contact vendor to regenerate the export; Silver table is NOT updated
If vendor cannot regenerate: inform operations team that today’s data is missing
All regions succeed but row count is low
Check Bronze file size; check filter logic for over-filtering
If root cause unclear: escalate to data quality review

blocks.content: What Comes Next

Module 4 shifts focus from the pipeline that writes data to the Warehouse that serves it. You have built production-grade pipelines that reliably move data from Bronze through Silver to Gold with error handling, parallel processing, and monitoring. Module 4 teaches how to design the Gold Warehouse’s tables for optimal analytical query performance – distribution strategies that align data placement with query patterns, statistics management for the query optimizer, materialized views for accelerating common queries, stored procedures for encapsulating Gold refresh logic, and row-level security for multi-team access control – so that the data your pipeline produces is served efficiently to NordGrid’s three consumer teams.

blocks.title: The pipeline is the heartbeat of the platform
blocks.content: NordGrid’s daily pipeline runs 365 days a year. It processes 438 million rows per year across 12 regions. It has failed 23 times in the past 6 months – and recovered automatically 19 of those times through retry logic and error isolation. The 4 times it required human intervention were genuine issues (vendor export failures, schema changes, capacity incidents) that no automation could have predicted. A pipeline with 82% automatic recovery is not perfect – but it means the on-call engineer is woken up 4 times per 6 months instead of 23. That is the production value of the patterns in this module.

chapters.title: Warehouse Design and Optimization
chapters.description: Design Fabric Warehouse tables for analytical query performance, optimize T-SQL queries with statistics and distribution strategies, and build cross-database architectures that serve multiple consumer teams efficiently.

blocks.content: In the junior-level track, the Fabric Warehouse was a read-write SQL store for Gold-layer aggregates. You created tables with CREATE TABLE, populated them with INSERT INTO...SELECT, and queried them with standard T-SQL. At junior scale – 4 regions, simple monthly aggregates, one consumer team – the Warehouse worked without tuning. At mid-level scale – 12 regions, 3 consumer teams running concurrent queries, complex multi-table joins for billing and regulatory reporting – the Warehouse’s default behavior produces performance problems that the junior-level track did not address.
What Changes at Scale

rows: Simple GROUP BY on one table
Multi-table joins with window functions and conditional aggregation
Complex queries expose suboptimal plans when statistics are stale
Concurrent queries compete for capacity – one slow query affects all others
Monthly billing: 240 rows (12 regions × 20 months). Detailed fact tables: millions of rows via cross-database query
Larger tables require distribution strategy and statistics management
Daily automated refresh via pipeline SQL activity
Frequent refreshes require stored procedures and maintenance scheduling
Regional analysts see only their region’s data
Row-level security predicates filter data transparently per user

blocks.content: Module Roadmap

Lessons 2–3 cover table design: distribution strategies that align data placement with query patterns, and statistics management that gives the query optimizer accurate information. Lessons 4–5 cover pre-computation: materialized views for transparent query acceleration and stored procedures for encapsulating multi-step Gold logic. Lesson 6 covers cross-database architecture for joining Lakehouse and Warehouse data efficiently. Lesson 7 covers window functions. Lesson 8 covers query performance diagnostics. Lesson 9 covers security with row-level and column-level access controls. Lesson 10 synthesizes everything into NordGrid’s Gold Warehouse architecture.

blocks.title: The Warehouse is the analyst’s interface to your engineering
blocks.content: The Silver transformation notebook and the Gold aggregation pipeline are invisible to analysts. What analysts see is the Warehouse: the tables they query, the views they depend on, the stored procedures that refresh their data. If the Warehouse is slow, poorly structured, or confusing, the analyst’s experience is poor – regardless of how elegant the upstream pipeline is.

When the Fabric Warehouse executes a query, it distributes the work across multiple compute nodes. How the table’s data is distributed across those nodes determines whether a join can be performed locally or requires data movement. The right distribution strategy eliminates data movement and makes joins fast. The wrong strategy forces the engine to shuffle millions of rows between nodes before the join can begin.
Distribution Options

The Fabric Warehouse supports three distribution strategies. Round-robin distributes rows evenly across nodes without considering column values. Hash distributes rows based on a specified column’s hash value – all rows with the same column value land on the same node. Replicated copies the entire table to every node. The choice depends on the table’s size, its role in joins, and how frequently it is updated.
Choosing the Right Strategy

rows: Hash on the most common join key
Co-locates data for the regional join that 90% of queries use
Too small for hash distribution to matter
Every node gets a copy – joins are always local
No joins expected – even distribution for parallel loading

blocks.title: Distribution matters for cross-database queries
blocks.content: NordGrid’s Gold Warehouse tables are small (under 10K rows). But the cross-database queries that join Silver Lakehouse data (millions of rows) with Gold dimension tables produce large intermediate results. When the dimension table is replicated, the join is local on every node – no data movement. For NordGrid’s regulatory query that joins 90 million Silver rows with 12 dimension rows, replicating the dimension eliminates a 400 MB data movement step that takes 15 seconds.

NordGrid’s quarterly regulatory query joined the Silver Lakehouse’s meter table (90 million rows) with the Gold Warehouse’s dim_regions table (12 rows). The expected behavior: the optimizer broadcasts the 12-row dimension table. The actual behavior: the optimizer chose a nested-loop join taking 45 seconds. The cause: stale statistics told the optimizer that dim_regions had 500,000 rows.
What Statistics Are

Statistics are metadata that the query optimizer uses to estimate row counts. Accurate statistics lead to good plans. Stale statistics lead to bad plans. The Fabric Warehouse creates statistics automatically on columns used in joins and filters, but automatic creation does not guarantee accuracy.
Updating Statistics

After running UPDATE STATISTICS, the estimated_rows for dim_regions dropped from 500,000 to 12, and the optimizer switched from a nested-loop join to a broadcast hash join. The query time dropped from 45 seconds to 3 seconds. NordGrid now runs sp_updatestats as the final step of every Gold Warehouse refresh pipeline.
When Statistics Go Stale

rows: Old statistics may persist with wrong row counts
Run UPDATE STATISTICS immediately after recreation
Statistics underestimate the new row count
Run UPDATE STATISTICS after the insert completes
Run UPDATE STATISTICS after the delete

blocks.title: Stale statistics are invisible until a query is slow
blocks.content: Unlike a missing index or a bad join condition, stale statistics do not produce error messages. The query runs correctly – it just runs 15x slower than it should. When a query suddenly becomes slow after a table refresh, check statistics first.

NordGrid’s analytics team runs the same monthly regional summary query dozens of times per day. Each execution scans the entire fact table and returns 12 rows per month. A materialized view pre-computes this aggregation and stores the result as a physical table. The query returns in under 100 milliseconds instead of 2 seconds.
What a Materialized View Is

A materialized view is a view whose query result is physically stored as data. When a user runs a query that matches the materialized view’s pattern, the optimizer can serve the result from the pre-computed data. The user does not need to reference the materialized view by name – the optimizer detects the match transparently.

CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW mv_monthly_region_summary AS
SELECT
    region,
    YEAR(date) AS year,
    MONTH(date) AS month,
    COUNT_BIG(*) AS reading_count,
    SUM(total_kw) AS sum_total_kw,
    SUM(meter_count) AS sum_meter_count
FROM fact_daily_consumption
GROUP BY region, YEAR(date), MONTH(date);
Refresh Strategies
Materialized views do not update automatically when the base table changes. NordGrid refreshes materialized views as the final step of the Gold refresh pipeline.
ALTER MATERIALIZED VIEW mv_monthly_region_summary REBUILD;
When to Materialize

left.label: Regular View (no materialization)
points: Recomputes on every query – always current, cost per query
Best for: queries run infrequently or with volatile base data
No storage cost
NordGrid use: ad-hoc analyst exploration queries

right.label: Materialized View
points: Pre-computed – stale until refreshed, near-zero cost per query
Best for: queries run frequently against stable base data
Consumes storage
NordGrid use: dashboard queries run 50+ times per day

blocks.title: Materialize the query, not the view name
blocks.content: Analysts do not need to know materialized views exist. They write queries against the base tables. The optimizer transparently matches their queries to the materialized view’s pre-computed result.

NordGrid’s Gold Warehouse refresh involves multiple sequential steps: delete the current month’s data, insert fresh aggregation, update statistics, and rebuild materialized views. Stored procedures encapsulate all of this in a single callable unit with error handling.
The Monthly Billing Refresh Procedure

blocks.title: THROW re-raises the error to the pipeline
blocks.content: The THROW statement inside the CATCH block is essential. Without it, the TRY/CATCH swallows the error – the procedure logs the failure and returns successfully, and the pipeline’s Script activity reports success. The pipeline’s failure notification never fires.

The junior-level track introduced cross-database queries. At mid-level, cross-database queries become architectural tools: the Gold Warehouse reads from multiple Lakehouses, serves as a unified query layer for consumers, and provides a single security-controlled access point.
The Unified Query Layer

CREATE OR ALTER VIEW v_detailed_consumption AS
SELECT
    s.date, s.region, d.region_name, d.country,
    s.customer_type, s.daily_kw, s.daily_mw,
    b.total_kw AS monthly_total_kw,
    ROUND(s.daily_kw / NULLIF(b.avg_kw, 0) * 100, 1) AS pct_of_avg
FROM lh_meter_readings.dbo.silver_meters s
INNER JOIN dw_nordgrid_analytics.dbo.dim_regions d ON s.region = d.region_code
LEFT JOIN dw_nordgrid_analytics.dbo.monthly_billing b
    ON s.region = b.region AND YEAR(s.date) = b.year AND MONTH(s.date) = b.month AND s.customer_type = b.customer_type;

blocks.title: Views are the API, cross-database is the implementation
blocks.content: Analysts query views. Views query tables – sometimes Warehouse-native, sometimes cross-database from Lakehouses. If performance requirements change, the data engineer changes the view definition. The analyst’s query does not change.

Window functions are the T-SQL feature that NordGrid’s analysts request most frequently. A window function computes a value across a set of rows related to the current row – without collapsing the result into a single row the way GROUP BY does.
The OVER Clause

-- Running total: cumulative energy consumption per region this month
SELECT date, region, total_kw,
    SUM(total_kw) OVER (
        PARTITION BY region ORDER BY date
        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS cumulative_kw
FROM fact_daily_consumption
WHERE year = 2025 AND month = 3
ORDER BY region, date;

-- Day-over-day change
SELECT date, region, total_kw,
    LAG(total_kw, 1) OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY date) AS prev_day_kw,
    total_kw - LAG(total_kw, 1) OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY date) AS daily_change
FROM fact_daily_consumption
WHERE year = 2025 AND month = 3;
Ranking Functions

-- Top 3 regions per day
WITH ranked AS (
    SELECT date, region, total_kw,
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY date ORDER BY total_kw DESC) AS rn
    FROM fact_daily_consumption WHERE year = 2025 AND month = 3
)
SELECT date, region, total_kw FROM ranked WHERE rn <= 3 ORDER BY date, rn;

blocks.title: Window functions are the analyst’s power tool
blocks.content: GROUP BY answers ‘what is the total per group?’ Window functions answer ‘how does each row relate to its group?’ NordGrid’s analytics team uses window functions in approximately 60% of their production queries.

Module 1 taught the diagnostic workflow for slow Spark notebooks. This lesson teaches the equivalent for slow Warehouse queries: read the T-SQL execution plan, identify expensive operators, check for missing statistics, and verify that materialized views are being used.
The Diagnostic Checklist

items: Check for Table Scan on large tables. A Table Scan on the Silver Lakehouse table indicates that partition pruning is not active.
Check estimated vs actual row counts. If they differ by 10x or more, statistics are stale.
Check the join strategy. A Nested Loops join between two large tables is almost always wrong.
Check for materialized view usage. The plan should show a scan of the materialized view instead of the base table.
Check for expensive sorts. An explicit ORDER BY on a large result set triggers a Sort operator that consumes significant memory.

blocks.title: The most common fix is the simplest one
blocks.content: In NordGrid’s experience, 60% of slow Warehouse queries are fixed by updating statistics. Another 20% are fixed by adding partition column filters to cross-database views. Start with the cheap fixes.

NordGrid’s three consumer teams should not all see the same data. The NORTH regional operations team should see only NORTH data. Row-level security (RLS) attaches a security predicate to a table, transparently filtering rows based on who is running the query.
Row-Level Security

CREATE FUNCTION fn_region_access(@region VARCHAR(20))
RETURNS TABLE
WITH SCHEMABINDING
AS
RETURN
    SELECT 1 AS access
    WHERE @region IN (
        SELECT region_code FROM dbo.user_region_access
        WHERE user_email = SYSTEM_USER
    )
    OR EXISTS (
        SELECT 1 FROM dbo.user_region_access
        WHERE user_email = SYSTEM_USER AND region_code = 'ALL'
    );

CREATE SECURITY POLICY region_filter
ADD FILTER PREDICATE dbo.fn_region_access(region)
    ON dbo.fact_daily_consumption
WITH (STATE = ON);

blocks.title: RLS does not protect against Lakehouse direct access
blocks.content: Row-level security is enforced in the Warehouse. If an analyst has direct access to the Silver Lakehouse’s SQL endpoint, they can bypass the Warehouse’s RLS. NordGrid addresses this by restricting Silver workspace Viewer access to the data engineering team only.
This lesson synthesizes everything into NordGrid’s production Gold Warehouse architecture: 8 tables, 2 materialized views, 3 stored procedures, 4 cross-database views, and RLS/column-level security.
The Maintenance Schedule

rows: Final step inside each stored procedure
Final step inside each stored procedure

blocks.content: What Comes Next
Module 5 introduces Fabric’s Real-Time Intelligence workload – the components that extend NordGrid’s architecture from batch-only to batch-plus-streaming.

blocks.title: The Warehouse is the platform’s front door
blocks.content: NordGrid’s consumers interact with the platform through the Warehouse. They do not see the Bronze Lakehouse, the Silver transformation notebook, or the Gold aggregation pipeline. A Warehouse that is fast, well-organized, and correctly secured earns trust.

chapters.title: Real-Time Intelligence
chapters.description: Understand Fabric’s Real-Time Intelligence workload, build Eventstream pipelines for continuous data capture, query streaming data with KQL, and connect real-time data to the medallion architecture.

blocks.content: NordGrid’s entire pipeline runs once per day at 02:00 UTC. The operations team sees yesterday’s data at 08:00. For meter readings, this 6-hour lag is acceptable. But NordGrid also operates 5,000 wind turbines that report SCADA telemetry every 10 seconds. A turbine bearing failure develops over 15 minutes. Real-time processing turns that 6-hour detection lag into a 30-second detection window.
Batch vs Real-Time Requirements

left.label: Batch Pipeline (Modules 1–4)
points: Data arrives once daily as a file (CSV, Parquet)
Processing runs on a schedule (02:00 UTC daily)
Latency: hours
Consumer: daily dashboards, monthly reports
Fabric tools: Pipeline, Notebook, Lakehouse, Warehouse

right.label: Real-Time Pipeline (This Module)
points: Data arrives continuously as events (every 10 seconds)
Processing runs continuously
Latency: seconds
Consumer: real-time operations dashboard, automated anomaly alerts
Fabric tools: Eventstream, KQL Database, Eventhouse, Real-Time Dashboard

blocks.content: Real-time processing does not replace batch processing – it complements it. NordGrid’s meter readings still flow through the daily batch pipeline. The SCADA telemetry flows through both: a real-time path for immediate anomaly detection and a batch path for historical analysis.

blocks.title: Real-time is a new skill set, not just a new tool
blocks.content: The batch pipeline uses PySpark, T-SQL, and the Data Factory canvas. Real-time intelligence introduces KQL (Kusto Query Language), a new storage engine (Eventhouse/KQL database), and a new mental model (continuous event streams rather than bounded file sets).

Before building anything, you need a clear map of the Real-Time Intelligence components and how they connect. This lesson provides that map – the four components (Eventstream, Eventhouse, KQL database, Real-Time Dashboard), their relationships, and their NordGrid implementations.
The Four Components

rows: Captures events from sources, applies in-flight transforms, routes to destinations
Stores streaming data with automatic indexing and retention; queried with KQL
Visualizes KQL query results with auto-refresh (every 30 seconds)

blocks.title: One Eventhouse, multiple KQL databases
blocks.content: An Eventhouse can host multiple KQL databases, just as a workspace can contain multiple Lakehouses. NordGrid uses one Eventhouse with two KQL databases: one for SCADA readings and one for operational events.

The Eventstream is the real-time equivalent of the pipeline’s Copy activity – it moves data from an external source into Fabric. NordGrid’s SCADA system publishes turbine telemetry to an Azure Event Hub at 5,000 events per 10-second window. The Eventstream connects to this Event Hub, receives events as they arrive, optionally transforms them in-flight, and routes them to one or more destinations.
In-Flight Transformations

Eventstreams can transform data before it reaches the destination – filtering out unwanted events, projecting specific fields. NordGrid’s Eventstream applies a filter that drops telemetry readings where turbine_status is ‘OFFLINE’ and a projection that selects only needed fields. These transformations reduce event volume by approximately 15% and event size by 40%.
Multi-Destination Routing

An Eventstream can route events to multiple destinations simultaneously. NordGrid routes to two: the KQL database for real-time querying and the Bronze Lakehouse for batch integration. Both destinations receive the same filtered and projected events.

blocks.title: Eventstream transformations are limited by design
blocks.content: Eventstream in-flight transformations support filter, project, and simple compute operations – but not joins, aggregations, or complex logic. If you need to enrich streaming events with reference data, the enrichment happens downstream in a KQL query.

The KQL database is to real-time data what the Lakehouse is to batch data – the managed storage layer where data lives, is indexed, and is queried. But the KQL database is designed for high-frequency writes, time-series queries, and retention-bounded storage.
Table Schema Definition

.create table turbine_telemetry (
    turbine_id: string,
    timestamp: datetime,
    rpm: real,
    temperature: real,
    vibration: real,
    power_output: real,
    region: string
)
Retention and Caching Policies

blocks.title: Retention is automatic
blocks.content: Unlike Delta tables (where VACUUM must be run manually), KQL databases enforce retention automatically. NordGrid’s SCADA telemetry generates 43 million rows per day. With 30-day retention, it stabilizes at approximately 1.3 billion rows.
KQL uses a pipe-based model where data flows through a chain of operators – similar to Unix pipes or PySpark method chaining.
The Pipe-Based Model

turbine_telemetry
| where timestamp > ago(1h)
| where region == "NORTH-1"
| project turbine_id, timestamp, rpm, temperature
| order by timestamp desc
| take 100
KQL vs T-SQL – Syntax Mapping

rows: SELECT region, AVG(rpm) GROUP BY region

blocks.content: Aggregation with summarize

turbine_telemetry
| where timestamp > ago(15m)
| summarize
    avg_rpm = avg(rpm),
    max_temp = max(temperature),
    turbine_count = dcount(turbine_id)
    by region
| order by region asc

blocks.title: KQL is optimized for the questions streaming data answers
blocks.content: KQL’s design reflects the questions that real-time data answers: ‘what happened in the last 15 minutes?’, ‘what is the average RPM right now?’ The ago() function, time-bucketing with bin(), and time-series functions are all purpose-built for time-bounded queries.

KQL’s real advantage emerges in time-series analysis: operations that treat data as a sequence of values over time and compute trends, anomalies, and forecasts. NordGrid uses time-series KQL to detect turbine degradation, identify production anomalies, and compute generation forecasts.
Time Bucketing with bin()

turbine_telemetry
| where timestamp > ago(6h)
| summarize
    avg_rpm = avg(rpm),
    avg_temp = avg(temperature),
    max_vibration = max(vibration)
    by turbine_id, bin(timestamp, 5m)
| order by turbine_id, timestamp
Anomaly Detection

turbine_telemetry
| where turbine_id == "T-00042"
| where timestamp > ago(24h)
| make-series avg_temp = avg(temperature)
    on timestamp step 5m
| extend anomalies = series_decompose_anomalies(avg_temp)
| mv-expand timestamp to typeof(datetime), avg_temp to typeof(real), anomalies to typeof(int)
| where anomalies != 0
Trend Detection

turbine_telemetry
| where timestamp > ago(24h)
| make-series avg_temp = avg(temperature)
    on timestamp step 1h
    by turbine_id
| extend (slope, intercept, r_squared) = series_fit_line(avg_temp)
| where slope > 0.5
| project turbine_id, slope, r_squared
| order by slope desc

blocks.title: make-series is the gateway to advanced analytics
blocks.content: Once data is in series format (arrays), KQL’s full library of time-series functions becomes available: series_decompose, series_fir, series_pearson_correlation, and series_fit_2lines.

sections.title: Connecting Real-Time to the Medallion Architecture

blocks.content: The real-time pipeline and the batch pipeline operate in parallel – processing the same SCADA telemetry data through different paths for different purposes. This lesson covers the integration patterns that connect the two paths into a unified data platform.
The Silver Consolidation Notebook

Every night at 02:00 UTC, a Silver consolidation notebook reads the day’s Parquet files from the Bronze Lakehouse, applies quality filters and schema enforcement, deduplicates, and writes to the Silver SCADA Delta table. The Silver table provides the long-term historical foundation for queries that span months or years.
Shared Transformation Logic

The Eventstream’s in-flight filter and the Silver notebook’s quality filter implement the same business rule in two different languages. NordGrid documents the shared rules in a single data quality specification that both reference. This duplication is an accepted trade-off of the dual-path architecture.

blocks.title: Deduplication at the Silver boundary
blocks.content: Because the Eventstream writes to the Bronze Lakehouse continuously and the Silver notebook runs daily, the Bronze Parquet files may contain duplicates from Eventstream delivery retries. The Silver consolidation notebook’s dropDuplicates on (turbine_id, timestamp) is essential.

The Real-Time Dashboard auto-refreshes at a configurable interval – every 30 seconds for NordGrid’s turbine operations view. The operations team monitors this dashboard on a wall-mounted display in the control room.
Dashboard Query Design

// Tile 1: Current average RPM per region
turbine_telemetry
| where timestamp > ago(5m)
| summarize avg_rpm = round(avg(rpm), 0) by region
| order by region

// Tile 2: Temperature trend over 6 hours
turbine_telemetry
| where timestamp > ago(6h)
| summarize avg_temp = avg(temperature) by bin(timestamp, 5m), region
| render timechart

// Tile 3: Turbines with anomalous temperature
turbine_telemetry
| where timestamp > ago(1h)
| summarize avg_temp = avg(temperature), max_temp = max(temperature) by turbine_id, region
| where max_temp > 75
| order by max_temp desc

blocks.title: Design dashboard queries for the refresh interval
blocks.content: Every dashboard query must complete in under 2 seconds (10% of the minimum practical refresh interval of 20 seconds). Queries that exceed 2 seconds are rewritten to use shorter time windows or coarser time buckets.

A batch pipeline runs once and either succeeds or fails. A streaming pipeline runs continuously – there is no single success or failure event. Streaming health is measured by continuous metrics: ingestion lag, throughput, and error rate.
Key Streaming Metrics

rows: Time between event generation and KQL availability
Events that could not be ingested

blocks.content: Monitoring with KQL

// Ingestion lag check
turbine_telemetry
| summarize max_event_time = max(timestamp)
| extend lag_seconds = datetime_diff('second', now(), max_event_time)
| project max_event_time, lag_seconds,
         status = iff(lag_seconds < 30, 'HEALTHY', iff(lag_seconds < 120, 'WARNING', 'CRITICAL'))

blocks.title: The batch path is the safety net
blocks.content: Real-time pipelines are inherently more fragile than batch pipelines. NordGrid’s architecture routes all streaming data through both paths. If the real-time path fails, the batch path continues to capture data. No data is lost.
This lesson synthesizes everything into NordGrid’s production real-time architecture.
The Complete Real-Time Stack

rows: Filter offline, project 7 fields, dual destination
Stores streaming data for real-time queries

blocks.content: Operational Metrics

After three months of production operation, NordGrid’s real-time pipeline processes an average of 43 million events per day. Dashboard query latency averages 400 milliseconds at P95. Ingestion lag averages 12 seconds. The streaming pipeline has experienced 4 disruptions in 3 months. Zero data was lost in any incident because the batch path captured all events.

blocks.title: Real-time extends the platform, it does not replace it
blocks.content: NordGrid’s real-time architecture adds four new Fabric item types to the existing platform – but the batch foundation remains the backbone. Real-time intelligence is a new capability layer that sits alongside the batch layer, serving use cases that batch cannot.

chapters.title: Testing Fabric Pipelines
chapters.description: Build a systematic testing practice for Fabric notebooks and pipelines, from unit tests for transformation functions through integration tests for complete pipeline runs, using data quality frameworks and validation patterns.

blocks.content: NordGrid’s Silver transformation notebook passed code review. The reviewer checked the filter logic, the schema definition, and the MERGE pattern. The notebook ran successfully in staging against 13,000 sample rows. On Monday night, it processed 1.2 million rows. On Tuesday morning, the billing team reported that residential meter counts had dropped by 23,000. The cause: the notebook’s deduplication used dropDuplicates(['meter_id']) instead of dropDuplicates(['meter_id', 'date']). The staging test did not catch it because the sample data had only one date. Three days of wrong billing data, two days of investigation, and half a day of reprocessing.
The Testing Pyramid

Unit tests at the base are numerous, fast, and cheap. Production monitoring at the top is continuous but catches problems late. Each level catches different defect types. NordGrid’s deduplication bug would have been caught at the unit test level – the cheapest and fastest level.
Module Roadmap

Lessons 2–3 cover unit testing: extracting transformation logic into testable functions and writing pytest tests. Lesson 4 covers schema validation. Lesson 5 covers data quality frameworks (Great Expectations). Lesson 6 covers integration testing. Lesson 7 covers testing non-notebook components. Lesson 8 covers regression testing. Lesson 9 covers test automation. Lesson 10 synthesizes NordGrid’s testing standards.

blocks.title: Testing is not optional at mid-level
blocks.content: At junior level, validation checks at the end of the notebook were the primary quality assurance mechanism. At mid-level, those checks are the last line of defense, not the first.

Fabric notebooks mix transformation logic with infrastructure code. The solution is to extract the transformation logic into standalone Python functions that take a DataFrame as input and return a DataFrame as output. These functions can be tested with pytest using small, controlled DataFrames.
Extracting Testable Functions
Writing the Tests

def test_dedup_keeps_different_dates(spark, sample_schema):
    data = [("M-001", 10.0, date(2025, 3, 22), "NORTH", "RES"),
            ("M-001", 12.0, date(2025, 3, 23), "NORTH", "RES")]
    df = spark.createDataFrame(data, schema=sample_schema)
    result = deduplicate_readings(df)
    assert result.count() == 2  # Same meter, different dates – both kept

blocks.title: Test the edge cases, not the happy path
blocks.content: NordGrid’s test suite has 45 test cases. Only 5 test the happy path. The other 40 test edge cases – each one representing a bug that either happened in production or was discovered during code review.

Unit tests for transformation functions need test data – small DataFrames with known values that exercise specific behaviors. Creating this test data is a skill: the data must be minimal (3–10 rows), targeted (each row exercises a specific edge case), and deterministic.
The Test Fixture Pattern

blocks.title: Start with 5 tests, not 50
blocks.content: A team with zero tests should not aim for 50 tests in the first week. Start with 5 tests that cover the most critical edge cases. NordGrid’s first 5 tests would have caught the 3 most expensive production incidents.

A transformation that produces the correct rows with the wrong schema is a production incident waiting to happen. Schema validation tests verify that the transformation’s output matches the expected column structure – names, types, nullability, and column order.
The Expected Schema Contract

# schema_contracts.py
SILVER_METERS_SCHEMA = StructType([
    StructField("meter_id", StringType(), nullable=False),
    StructField("daily_kw", DoubleType(), nullable=False),
    StructField("date", DateType(), nullable=False),
    StructField("region", StringType(), nullable=False),
    StructField("customer_type", StringType(), nullable=False),
    StructField("year", IntegerType(), nullable=False),
    StructField("month", IntegerType(), nullable=False),
    StructField("daily_mw", DoubleType(), nullable=False),
    StructField("processing_timestamp", TimestampType(), nullable=False),
])

blocks.title: Schema tests catch drift before production catches it
blocks.content: Without schema tests, schema drift is discovered at write time (the MERGE fails) or – worse – not at all (mergeSchema silently adds a column). Schema tests catch drift at development time.

Data quality tests verify that the actual production output meets quantitative expectations: row count within a range, null rates below a threshold, value distributions within expected bounds, and uniqueness constraints satisfied.
NordGrid’s Expectation Suite

import great_expectations as gx

validator = context.sources.pandas_default.read_dataframe(df_final.toPandas())
validator.expect_table_row_count_to_be_between(min_value=450000, max_value=550000)
validator.expect_column_values_to_not_be_null("meter_id")
validator.expect_column_values_to_not_be_null("daily_kw")
validator.expect_column_values_to_be_between("daily_kw", min_value=0, max_value=10000)
validator.expect_compound_columns_to_be_unique(["meter_id", "date"])

results = validator.validate()

blocks.title: Expectations are living documentation
blocks.content: The expectation suite is not just a test – it is the most accurate documentation of what ‘correct data’ means for the Silver table. When a new engineer asks ‘what row count should the Silver table have?’, the answer is in the expectation suite.

Unit tests verify individual functions. Schema tests verify output structure. Data quality tests verify value correctness. But none of these test the pipeline as a whole. Integration tests fill this gap by running the complete pipeline in a staging workspace against representative data and verifying end-to-end behavior.
The Integration Test Workflow

items: Reset the staging environment: delete existing Silver and Gold tables. Upload a known Bronze file with controlled edge cases.
Run the complete pipeline in the staging workspace with the staging environment parameter.
Verify the Silver table: check row count, null counts, region completeness, and schema against the contract.
Verify the Gold Warehouse: check that monthly_billing has expected rows and refresh_log shows SUCCESS.
Verify pipeline_metrics: check that the table has a record with correct values.
Report results to a test_results table for historical tracking.

blocks.title: The staging workspace must mirror production structure
blocks.content: An integration test is only valuable if the staging environment accurately mirrors production. NordGrid maintains the staging workspace using the same deployment pipeline as production.

NordGrid’s pipeline includes non-notebook components that also need testing: Dataflow Gen2 items, SQL views, and Warehouse stored procedures. These components cannot be tested with pytest because they are not Python code.
Testing SQL Views

blocks.title: Use test-specific data markers
blocks.content: NordGrid’s SQL tests use region codes like 'TEST-1' and 'TEST-2' that never appear in production data. This makes test data instantly distinguishable and enables precise cleanup.

A golden dataset is a fixed set of input rows with a known correct output. Every time the transformation code changes, the regression test runs the input through the new code and compares the output to the golden reference. If the output differs, the developer determines whether the difference is intentional or a regression.
The Golden Dataset Pattern

def test_regression_against_golden_dataset(spark):
    df_input = spark.read.parquet("tests/golden/input.parquet")
    df_expected = spark.read.parquet("tests/golden/expected_output.parquet")
    df_actual = full_transform(df_input)

    diff_actual = df_actual.subtract(df_expected)
    diff_expected = df_expected.subtract(df_actual)

    assert diff_actual.count() == 0, f"{diff_actual.count()} unexpected rows"
    assert diff_expected.count() == 0, f"{diff_expected.count()} missing rows"

blocks.title: Regression tests catch what other tests miss
blocks.content: Unit tests verify individual functions. Schema tests verify structure. Regression tests verify the complete output against a known-good reference. They are the only test type that catches emergent bugs – problems caused by the interaction between transformation steps.
Tests that exist but are not run are the same as tests that do not exist. Test automation ensures that tests run automatically as part of the development workflow.
The Test Notebook

# nb_run_tests – the test gate notebook
import pytest

exit_code = pytest.main([
    "-v", "--tb=short", "-x",
    "tests/",
    f"--junitxml=Files/test_results/results_{processing_date}.xml"
])

if exit_code != 0:
    raise Exception(f"Tests failed with exit code {exit_code}. Pipeline halted.")
The Pipeline Gate Pattern

NordGrid’s production pipeline runs the test notebook before the transformation notebook. If the tests fail, the ForEach never runs. This gate adds approximately 15 seconds to the pipeline runtime – a negligible cost for the protection it provides.

blocks.title: The test notebook needs the same environment as the transform notebook
blocks.content: If the test notebook uses a different Fabric environment than the transform notebook, the tests may pass in one environment and fail in the other. NordGrid ensures both notebooks use the same workspace default environment.
This lesson synthesizes everything into NordGrid’s testing standards – the minimum requirements, the coverage targets, and the review criteria.
Minimum Test Requirements

rows: One test per quality filter rule, one per derived column, one per dedup business key
Output schema matches contract for every Silver and Gold table
Row count range, null checks on required columns, uniqueness on business key
Golden dataset comparison for the complete Silver transformation
Complete pipeline run in staging with known data
Output comparison for every production view and dataflow

blocks.content: The Code Review Testing Checklist

items: New transformation logic has corresponding unit tests.
Schema contract is updated if the output schema changed.
Golden dataset is updated if the transformation output changed intentionally.
Test gate notebook passes in staging workspace.
No skipped or commented-out tests.

blocks.title: Testing is the practice that makes everything else reliable
blocks.content: NordGrid’s test suite runs in 15 seconds and has prevented 12 production incidents in 6 months. That is 15 seconds well spent.

chapters.title: Deployment and CI/CD
chapters.description: Implement Git-based version control for Fabric items, build deployment pipelines that promote changes from development through staging to production, and establish rollback procedures for failed deployments.

blocks.content: NordGrid’s data engineer needed to test a change to the Silver transformation notebook. The fastest path: open the production notebook, edit it, run the cells manually, save. That night the scheduled pipeline ran the modified notebook with unexpected results. The production notebook had been edited directly, bypassing code review, testing, staging, and every safeguard the team had built. CI/CD prevents this by making the Git repository the single source of truth for all production items.
The Problem with Manual Deployment

rows: Changes bypass code review and testing
All changes go through Git – PR – review – staging – production
Deploy by saving in the portal
Git preserves every version; deployment pipeline can revert
Same code promoted through identical environments
Git log records every change with author and timestamp

blocks.title: Direct production edits are the root cause of most deployment incidents
blocks.content: NordGrid tracked 8 deployment-related incidents over 6 months. Six were caused by direct production edits. After implementing CI/CD, direct-edit incidents dropped to zero because the production workspace’s Git integration uses a protected branch that only the deployment pipeline can write to.

At mid-level, Git integration becomes the foundation of the entire deployment process. Every Fabric item that can be serialized lives in Git. The Git repository is the source of truth: the workspace is a deployment of the repository’s contents, not the other way around.
The Branching Strategy

NordGrid’s repository has three long-lived branches: develop, staging, and main. Feature branches merge into develop via pull request (code review required). Develop merges into staging via automated promotion. Staging merges into main via gated promotion (integration tests must pass). Each branch is connected to a workspace that syncs when the branch changes.
The Pull Request Workflow

NordGrid requires every change to go through a pull request before merging into develop. The PR workflow: create a feature branch, make changes, open a PR, request review. Automated checks run the test suite. NordGrid’s PR merge policy requires at least one approval and zero test failures.

blocks.title: Git is the single source of truth
blocks.content: If a production notebook looks different from the Git repository’s main branch, the workspace is wrong and must be re-synced from Git. The answer is always: the Git branch connected to that workspace is the correct version.

Fabric provides a built-in deployment pipeline feature for promoting workspace items across environments. A deployment pipeline defines stages (development, staging, production), maps each stage to a workspace, and handles item promotion with deployment rules that remap environment-specific references.
Deployment Rules

rows: Notebook reads/writes point to production Lakehouse
Stored procedure calls use production Warehouse
Python libraries and Spark settings match production
Configuration table lookup reads production values

blocks.title: Test deployment rules in staging before production
blocks.content: A misconfigured deployment rule can cause the production notebook to read from the development Lakehouse. NordGrid tests deployment rules by first promoting from dev to staging and verifying all references were remapped correctly.

The deployment pipeline promotes Fabric items but does not automatically promote Fabric environments – the Python library specifications and Spark configuration. NordGrid’s solution is environment-as-code: a requirements file stored in Git alongside the notebooks, listing every Python library with a pinned version.
Environment-as-Code

# environments/requirements_prod.txt
great-expectations==0.18.8
pyarrow==14.0.2
nordgrid-utils==1.3.0
pandas==2.2.1
numpy==1.26.4
delta-spark==3.1.0

The requirements file is version-controlled, reviewed in PRs, and deployed programmatically. The deployment script reads the file, compares it to the target environment’s current libraries, and publishes any differences before promoting notebooks.

blocks.title: Pin library versions, do not use latest
blocks.content: Unpinned versions allow the dependency resolver to install different versions in different environments. Pinned versions guarantee that every environment produces the same set of libraries.

NordGrid operates 9 Fabric workspaces. Keeping 6 environments synchronized is an operational challenge. The key design decision: staging and production use the same requirements file. When a library is promoted from dev to production, it is added to requirements_prod.txt in a PR.
Automated Environment Publishing

NordGrid’s deployment automation includes an environment publishing step that runs before item promotion. The script reads the target environment’s requirements file, compares it to the currently published environment, and publishes a new version if differences exist. This sequence is critical: promoting a notebook before the environment has the required libraries causes the production pipeline to fail.

blocks.title: Environment publishing is slow – plan for it
blocks.content: Publishing a Fabric environment takes 3–8 minutes. NordGrid schedules environment-changing deployments during the maintenance window rather than during rapid-deployment windows for code-only hotfixes.

When a production deployment introduces a problem, the team needs a rollback procedure. NordGrid uses three mechanisms: Git revert (code rollback in 2 minutes), deployment pipeline revert (for deployment rule issues), and Delta time travel RESTORE (for data rollback).
NordGrid’s Rollback Playbook

items: Assess the impact: which tables are affected, how long has the problematic code been running.
Stop the bleeding: disable the production pipeline’s schedule.
Roll back the code: execute a Git revert and sync the production workspace.
Roll back the data: RESTORE the Silver table to the last known-good version using Delta time travel.
Re-run the pipeline with corrected code.
Communicate to affected consumer teams.
Post-mortem: identify root cause and preventive measures.

blocks.title: Practice rollbacks before you need them
blocks.content: NordGrid runs a rollback drill quarterly: the team deliberately deploys a known-bad change to staging, executes the full rollback playbook, and measures time from detection to resolution. Target: under 30 minutes.

NordGrid stores workspace configuration in a dedicated directory within the Git repository: config/workspaces/. Each workspace has a JSON file that defines its settings: capacity SKU, Spark pool configuration, default environment name, Git branch connection, and pipeline schedule definitions. When a setting needs to change, the engineer updates the JSON file and submits a PR.
What to Treat as Configuration

rows: A capacity change affects pipeline performance
Pool size and timeout affect notebook execution

blocks.title: Configuration drift is invisible until it causes an incident
blocks.content: Unlike code bugs (which produce errors) or data bugs (which produce wrong results), configuration drift is silent. Version-controlled configuration makes drift detectable: the deployment script compares actual settings to expected settings and reports differences.

NordGrid uses Azure DevOps Pipelines to automate deployment triggers. When a PR is merged to develop, a DevOps pipeline runs tests, promotes to staging, runs integration tests, requests approval, then promotes to production. The entire flow takes 20–30 minutes from PR merge to production.
The Manual Approval Gate

NordGrid deliberately keeps one manual step: the production approval gate. The deployment lead reviews which items are being promoted, what changed, and whether the timing is appropriate. The approval typically takes 5 minutes.

blocks.title: Automate the mechanical, gate the consequential
blocks.content: Running tests is mechanical – a machine does it faster. Promoting to staging is mechanical. Promoting to production is consequential – it affects real data and real consumers. NordGrid automates the mechanical steps and gates the consequential ones.

Some changes span multiple items that must be deployed together: a schema change requires simultaneous updates to the notebook, pipeline, stored procedure, SQL views, and semantic model. All changed items are included in one promotion. The pipeline schedule is paused during the 5-minute promotion window.
The Schema Change Example

items: Engineer creates a feature branch and makes all changes: notebook, pipeline, stored procedure, SQL views, schema contract tests, golden dataset.
All changes are in a single PR. The reviewer verifies every downstream item is updated.
After PR merge, automated deployment promotes all items to staging simultaneously.
After staging validation, the deployment lead approves production promotion.
After promotion, the pipeline schedule is re-enabled.

blocks.title: Pause the pipeline schedule during multi-item promotions
blocks.content: If the production pipeline runs during a multi-item promotion – after the notebook is updated but before the stored procedure is updated – the pipeline may succeed partially, creating cascading failures.
This lesson synthesizes everything into NordGrid’s CI/CD standards: deployment flow, policies, and the discipline that makes velocity safe.
The Deployment Policies

items: All production changes go through Git. No direct editing of production items.
Every PR requires at least one reviewer and zero test failures.
Environment changes require the same review as code changes.
Multi-item changes are deployed atomically.
Rollback is a documented, practiced procedure. Target: under 30 minutes.
Post-deployment monitoring continues for 48 hours.

blocks.content: The Deployment Flow Summary

rows: At least 1 approval, zero test failures

blocks.title: CI/CD is the discipline that makes velocity safe
blocks.content: Without CI/CD, moving fast means breaking things. With CI/CD, moving fast is safe. NordGrid deploys to production 3 times per week. Each deployment completes in 25 minutes with zero human involvement after the approval gate.

chapters.title: Capacity Management and Cost Optimization
chapters.description: Understand Fabric’s capacity model in production depth, monitor and optimize capacity consumption across workloads, implement workload isolation strategies, and establish cost allocation practices for multi-team environments.

blocks.content: At mid-level scale – 3 teams, 12 production pipelines, concurrent analyst queries, Power BI refreshes, and a streaming Eventstream – capacity becomes an operational constraint. NordGrid’s mid-level capacity crisis: the overnight Silver notebook took 38 minutes instead of the usual 14 because the monthly billing Power BI refresh ran simultaneously, consuming 40% of the F64 capacity.
The Capacity Model

Fabric’s capacity model provides a pool of Capacity Units (CUs) shared by all workloads. When total demand exceeds the pool’s capacity, Fabric throttles lower-priority workloads. Throttling is a design feature that prevents monopolization, but throttling that delays a production pipeline beyond its SLA is an operational problem.
NordGrid’s Capacity Inventory

blocks.title: Capacity is shared by design, not by accident
blocks.content: The shared capacity model is Fabric’s core economic proposition: instead of purchasing dedicated compute for each workload, you purchase one capacity pool that all workloads share. Capacity management ensures the peaks do not overlap.
Fabric provides a capacity metrics app – a Power BI-based monitoring tool that shows CU consumption over time, broken down by workload type, workspace, and individual item.
Key Metrics to Monitor

rows: Maximum CU usage during peak period
Number of times workloads were queued
Additional wait time for interactive queries during throttling
Additional wait time for background jobs

blocks.title: Check the metrics app after every SLA breach
blocks.content: When a pipeline exceeds its SLA, check the capacity metrics first. If the CU timeline shows throttling, the slowdown is capacity-related, not code-related. Optimizing code that is being throttled does not help.
NordGrid uses two isolation mechanisms: capacity separation (different workspaces on different capacities) and scheduling separation (ensuring high-consumption workloads do not overlap in time).
Capacity Separation

NordGrid purchased a second capacity (F32) dedicated to analytics and Power BI. The data engineering workspaces remain on the F64. Now the Silver notebook has the full F64 at 02:00, and the Power BI refresh runs on the F32 without affecting the pipeline.

left.label: Single Capacity (Before)
points: F64: all workloads share one pool
Peak CU demand: 80–95% when overlapping
Throttling: 2–3 events per week
Silver notebook: 14–38 minutes (variable)
Cost: 1 × F64

right.label: Split Capacities (After)
points: F64: data engineering only
Peak CU demand: 55–60% (no contention)
Throttling: 0 events per week
Silver notebook: 13–15 minutes (stable)
Cost: 1 × F64 + 1 × F32 (60% increase)

blocks.content: The 70% Rule
NordGrid’s scheduling rule: the sum of all concurrent workloads’ expected CU consumption must not exceed 70% of the capacity’s CU limit. The 30% headroom absorbs variability.

blocks.title: Scheduling separation is fragile
blocks.content: Scheduling separation works when workloads finish on time. NordGrid uses pipeline dependencies rather than fixed schedules: the Gold notebook starts after the Silver notebook completes, not at a fixed time.
Capacity optimization adds a dimension beyond performance optimization: the question is not just ‘how fast?’ but ‘how many CUs while running?’
Optimization Techniques

rows: spark.stop() at notebook end; session timeout reduced to 5 min
All production notebooks end with spark.stop()
Profile with different executor counts; choose cost-efficient point
Silver notebook: 4 executors instead of default 8
select() only needed columns before joins
Enable V-Order for Delta table writes
Net negative on write, saves on downstream reads

blocks.title: Profile before optimizing
blocks.content: The optimizations have different returns depending on the notebook’s profile. A notebook that is shuffle-heavy benefits most from column pruning. A notebook that leaves a 20-minute idle session benefits most from explicit session stop. Profile first, then target the dominant phase.
A pipeline’s total CU cost is the sum of its activities’ individual costs plus orchestration overhead. NordGrid’s daily pipeline consumes approximately 180 CU-minutes per run.
Incremental Loads vs Full Rebuilds

The single largest CU optimization was the transition from full overwrite to MERGE. The full overwrite consumed approximately 450 CU-minutes. The MERGE consumes approximately 120 CU-minutes – a 73% savings from a pipeline design change that eliminated unnecessary data processing.
Pipeline CU Budget

NordGrid assigns a CU budget to each production pipeline. If a pipeline run exceeds its budget, the pipeline_metrics table records the overage. The budget prevents gradual CU creep as data volume grows and new transformation steps are added.

blocks.title: The cheapest CU is the one you do not consume
blocks.content: NordGrid’s CU optimization program reduced the daily pipeline’s consumption by 60% over 6 months, entirely through architectural and configuration changes – no code logic was modified.

NordGrid’s 15 analysts run an average of 200 SQL queries per day. Each query is individually small but 200 queries at 2 CU-minutes average equals 400 CU-minutes per day – more than double the pipeline’s 180 CU-minutes.
Result Caching
The Warehouse caches query results. If an analyst runs the same query within the cache window, the cached result is returned without re-executing – consuming near-zero CUs.
Routing Queries to the Right Engine

blocks.title: Educate analysts, do not restrict them
blocks.content: NordGrid’s analyst CU consumption dropped 40% after the first CU awareness summary – not because of technical changes, but because analysts made better query choices when they understood the cost.

Without cost allocation, the Fabric capacity bill is a single line item charged to the platform budget. Cost allocation makes consumption visible by attributing CU costs to the teams and pipelines that consume them.
The Monthly Cost Report

blocks.title: Chargeback is a governance decision, not a technical one
blocks.content: Some organizations charge CU costs directly to team budgets (chargeback). Others use CU reports for awareness without billing (showback). NordGrid uses showback because their three teams are cooperative – awareness motivates optimization without the overhead of a billing system.

There are two responses to capacity constraints: optimize (reduce consumption) and scale (buy more capacity). Scaling is appropriate when the data volume, team size, or workload complexity has grown beyond what optimization can address.
SKU Upgrade vs Additional Capacity

left.label: SKU Upgrade (F64 → F128)
points: One larger pool – all workloads share more resources
Simpler management – one capacity to monitor
No isolation – a runaway query still affects the pipeline
Cost: 2x the F64 cost
Best for: growing workloads without contention

right.label: Additional Capacity (F64 + F32)
points: Two separate pools – workloads isolated
More complex – two capacities to manage
Full isolation – analytics and engineering separated
Cost: 1.5x the F64 cost
Best for: contention between workload types

blocks.content: Growth Planning

NordGrid’s quarterly capacity planning process projects the next 12 months of CU demand. If the projection shows demand will exceed 70% within 6 months, the platform team initiates a scaling decision proactively.

blocks.title: Scale before you are forced to
blocks.content: Reactive scaling takes days to weeks. Proactive scaling can be planned during a maintenance window with no user impact. NordGrid’s last two scaling decisions were both implemented proactively, with zero SLA breaches.
Fabric’s capacity model includes a burst allowance: the capacity can temporarily exceed its nominal CU limit by drawing from a burst pool that accumulates during low-utilization periods.
How Burst Works

Every minute the capacity is underutilized, it deposits CUs into the burst pool. When demand exceeds the nominal rate, the capacity withdraws from the burst pool. If the burst pool is depleted and demand is still high, throttling begins.
Smoothing and Overages

Fabric bills capacity based on a smoothed consumption rate – the average CU consumption over a sliding window (typically 5 minutes). This smoothing means that a brief spike does not immediately trigger throttling.

blocks.title: Burst is free headroom – but it is finite
blocks.content: NordGrid’s architecture exploits burst by scheduling heavy workloads during off-peak hours, accumulating burst during idle time, and consuming burst during the pipeline spike. This schedule-aware design effectively gives NordGrid more capacity than the F64’s nominal CU rate.
This lesson synthesizes everything into NordGrid’s capacity governance – the processes, metrics, and decision frameworks that keep the Fabric platform performing well and costing appropriately.
The Weekly Capacity Review

Every Monday, the platform team spends 15 minutes reviewing: peak CU utilization (target: below 70%), throttling event count (target: zero), per-team CU trend, burst pool status, and pipeline SLA headroom.
The Capacity Dashboard

blocks.title: Capacity governance is financial governance
blocks.content: Capacity is not just a technical resource – it is a financial commitment. Every CU consumed by an unoptimized notebook or an idle Spark session is money spent without proportional value. The data engineer who reduces consumption by 30% is saving the organization money.

chapters.title: Multi-Team Governance and Security
chapters.description: Design workspace hierarchies for multi-team organizations, implement fine-grained OneLake security, enforce governance policies through Microsoft Purview, and establish operational standards for shared Fabric environments.

blocks.content: At mid-level, NordGrid has three teams – data engineering (5 people), analytics (7 people), and ML (3 people) – sharing the same Fabric tenant. Without governance, the analytics team creates workspaces without naming conventions, the ML team copies production data to experimental workspaces without sensitivity labels, and nobody knows which workspaces contain certified production data versus experimental prototypes.
What Goes Wrong Without Governance

rows: Workspace naming conventions enforced by domain policies
Analysts given Admin access because it was easiest
Role assignment reviews with least-privilege principle
ML team copies customer data to unprotected workspace
Analytics team creates own Silver tables
Compliance team cannot determine who accessed customer data
Purview audit logs and access reviews

blocks.title: Governance enables sharing, it does not restrict it
blocks.content: Without governance, NordGrid would restrict Silver data access to the data engineering team only. With governance – role-based access, sensitivity labels, row-level security, audit logs – NordGrid can share Silver data with all three teams confidently.

NordGrid uses the hybrid pattern: layer-based workspaces for shared production data (ws_nordgrid_bronze, ws_nordgrid_silver, ws_nordgrid_gold) owned by data engineering, plus team-based workspaces for team-specific work (ws_nordgrid_analytics, ws_nordgrid_ml, ws_nordgrid_platform).
Three Design Patterns

rows: One workspace per medallion layer per environment
Small organizations with one engineering team
Layer-based for shared production data, team-based for team-specific work
Multi-team organizations with shared medallion architecture

blocks.content: Workspace Naming Convention
The pattern is ws_{org}_{environment}_{purpose}, where the environment segment is dropped for production. This convention makes workspaces sortable, identifiable, and searchable.

blocks.title: Workspace proliferation is the silent governance failure
blocks.content: NordGrid’s rule: every workspace must have an owner, a documented purpose, and a review date. NordGrid’s quarterly workspace review identifies orphaned workspaces and archives or deletes them.
Fabric’s security model has three layers: workspace roles (coarse), OneLake data access roles (medium – which tables a user can read), and row-level/column-level security in the Warehouse (fine).
The Security Matrix

blocks.title: OneLake security applies to the Lakehouse, not the SQL endpoint
blocks.content: OneLake data access roles restrict access through OneLake paths. They do not automatically restrict access through the SQL analytics endpoint. NordGrid configures security in both layers for consistent boundaries.
NordGrid’s ML team needs meter consumption patterns but does not need customer_type. Dynamic data masking replaces column values with a mask pattern for users who do not have UNMASK permission.
Dynamic Data Masking

blocks.title: Mask at the earliest shared layer
blocks.content: NordGrid applies column masking on the Silver Lakehouse’s SQL endpoint – the earliest layer that all consumer teams access. By masking at the Silver layer, the protection follows the data regardless of the access path.

Microsoft Purview adds a classification layer: what kind of data is it, how sensitive is it, and what policies apply to it? Sensitivity labels classify Fabric items by sensitivity level and trigger automated protection policies.
Sensitivity Labels
NordGrid uses three labels: General (non-sensitive aggregates), Confidential (meter_id and customer_type), and Highly Confidential (billing details and financial projections).
Protection Policies

blocks.title: Labels must be applied to be effective
blocks.content: Purview’s protection policies only apply to items that have sensitivity labels. An unlabeled Lakehouse has no export restrictions. NordGrid’s quarterly security review checks every production item for label presence.

Fabric domains are organizational containers that group related workspaces under a common governance umbrella. NordGrid uses domains to separate production operations from experimental work and to enforce default sensitivity labels.
NordGrid’s Domain Structure

rows: Any team member – auto-deleted after 90 days

blocks.title: Domains scale governance without scaling the platform team
blocks.content: Without domains, every governance decision flows through the platform team. With domains, governance is delegated to domain administrators who understand their team’s needs.

NordGrid’s OneLake catalog shows 47 items. A new analyst finds three items named ‘meter readings’. Without endorsement labels, they cannot distinguish the authoritative production data from team-specific copies. Endorsement levels – Promoted and Certified – solve this.
The Certification Checklist

items: Sensitivity label applied with correct classification.
Documentation complete: data domain, owner, refresh schedule, upstream source.
Pipeline monitored with failure notifications and SLA tracking.
Tests passing consistently for at least 30 days.
Security configured and tested with impersonation for all consumer teams.
Maintenance scheduled: Delta OPTIMIZE/VACUUM running successfully.
Schema contract defined and validated by automated tests.

blocks.title: Certification builds trust, and trust enables adoption
blocks.content: After the engineering team achieved Certified status on the Silver Lakehouse, the analytics team decommissioned their shadow tables. The Certified badge communicated reliability with verifiable evidence rather than verbal assurance.
