NordGrid’s energy regulator requires annual proof that customer data is accessed only by authorized personnel. Fabric and Purview provide this evidence through unified audit logs.
What Gets Logged

rows: User opened a Lakehouse, ran a SQL query
Proves only authorized users accessed sensitive data
User downloaded a CSV, exported a report
Proves DLP policies prevented unauthorized export
Admin granted Viewer access to a new analyst
RLS predicate modified, column mask added

blocks.content: Automated Compliance Reporting

NordGrid automates monthly compliance reports using a Fabric notebook that queries the audit log API and writes a summary with: count of Confidential item accesses per user, list of permission changes, list of label changes, and any anomalous access patterns.

blocks.title: Audit is the proof that governance works
blocks.content: Governance policies are promises. Audit logs are proof. NordGrid’s compliance team does not trust policies alone – they trust policies verified by audit evidence.
Governance is also the operational standards that keep the platform navigable, maintainable, and onboardable for new team members.
NordGrid’s Operational Standards

rows: Prefix by type: lh_, nb_, pl_, dw_, sm_, es_
Every production item has description with owner and refresh schedule
Classified as additive/type-widening/breaking; follow the workflow
CI/CD pipeline enforces schema contract tests
Every pipeline has a named owner and backup owner

blocks.content: Runbook Library
NordGrid maintains a runbook library – documented procedures for common operational tasks. Every production pipeline must have a corresponding runbook before going live.

blocks.title: Write the runbook when you build the pipeline, not when it breaks
blocks.content: The best time to write a runbook is during pipeline development. NordGrid includes runbook creation as a step in the pipeline promotion checklist.
This lesson synthesizes everything into NordGrid’s governance framework: the four-pillar system that keeps a 3-team, 15-person Fabric environment secure, compliant, navigable, and trustworthy.
The Governance Calendar

blocks.title: Governance is the platform’s immune system
blocks.content: Without governance, every new team member, workload, and dataset introduces risk. Governance does not prevent growth – it makes growth safe. NordGrid added 5 team members and 3 new data sources in the past year with zero security incidents or compliance gaps.

chapters.title: The Production Fabric Platform
chapters.description: Operate a complete Fabric platform in production: incident management, monitoring dashboards, operational runbooks, platform evolution planning, and the transition from building pipelines to designing systems.

blocks.content: Building a pipeline is a project with a beginning and an end. Operating a platform is a practice without an end. The pipeline runs every night at 02:00 UTC. It will run tomorrow night. And every night for the next three years. The mid-level transition is from building things that work to operating things that keep working.
Development vs Operations

left.label: Development Mindset
points: Success = the pipeline runs correctly once
Focus: make it work, then make it fast
Failure is a bug to fix during development
Quality is measured by code correctness
The work ends when the pipeline is deployed

right.label: Operations Mindset
points: Success = the pipeline runs correctly every night for 365 nights
Focus: make it work, make it fast, then keep it working at scale over time
Failure is an incident to manage in production
Quality is measured by SLIs: freshness, latency, error rate, availability
The work begins when the pipeline is deployed

blocks.content: NordGrid’s SLIs and SLOs

rows: Time since last successful Silver MERGE
% of daily runs with status SUCCESS
95th percentile response time for Gold queries
Time between turbine event and KQL availability

blocks.title: Error budgets prevent both under-engineering and over-engineering
blocks.content: A 99% success rate SLO explicitly says: 3 failures per year are acceptable. Error budgets turn the abstract question ‘is our platform reliable enough?’ into the quantitative question ‘have we consumed our error budget?’

NordGrid’s morning check dashboard answers five questions in order of urgency: did last night’s pipeline succeed? Is the data fresh? Are all regions present? Did the Warehouse refresh complete? Any capacity throttling overnight?
The Morning Check Dashboard

The dashboard is designed for a 2-minute review at 08:00 UTC. Five green/red indicators sourced from the pipeline_metrics, refresh_log, and capacity metrics. The on-call engineer opens the dashboard, scans the indicators, and either confirms ‘all green’ or escalates.
The Weekly Trend Dashboard

The morning check shows point-in-time status. The weekly trend dashboard shows seven-day trends: pipeline duration, region failure rate, CU consumption, query latency percentiles, and Eventstream lag. Trends reveal problems that point-in-time checks miss.

blocks.title: Design dashboards for the decision they support
blocks.content: The morning check supports a binary decision (healthy yes/no). The weekly trend supports an analytical decision (where to invest optimization effort). Two separate dashboards because the decisions need different information.

NordGrid’s pipeline fails at 02:07 UTC. The ForEach completes 11 of 12 regions, but SOUTH-2 fails with a schema mismatch. The billing team needs SOUTH-2 data by 09:00. The on-call engineer has 90 minutes to diagnose, fix, and reprocess.
The Incident Lifecycle

items: Detection: automated alert fires to Slack. Target: all severity-1 incidents detected within 15 minutes.
Triage: classify by severity. Severity 1: consumer-facing impact with SLA breach imminent.
Response: diagnose root cause, implement fix, reprocess. Target: severity-1 resolved within 60 minutes.
Communication: update Slack with status. Notify affected consumer teams if data will be late.
Post-mortem: document root cause, detection time, response time, impact, and preventive measures.

blocks.content: NordGrid’s Incident Log

Every incident is recorded in the incident_log table. NordGrid’s log shows that 40% of incidents were schema-related, 25% capacity-related, and 20% data quality issues. This distribution guided the team’s investment priorities.

blocks.title: Every incident deserves a post-mortem, not just the severe ones
blocks.content: The post-mortem is not a blame exercise – it is a learning document. NordGrid’s schema validation step (which now alerts within 5 minutes of a schema change) exists because a post-mortem identified the gap.

A good runbook is not documentation – it is a decision tree that tells the engineer exactly what to do for a specific scenario, with commands to run, checks to perform, and escalation paths to follow.
Runbook Structure

Every NordGrid runbook follows six sections: Trigger (what activates this runbook), Impact assessment (what consumers are affected), Diagnostic steps (specific checks in order), Resolution steps (actions for each root cause), Escalation (when and to whom), and Verification (checks to confirm the fix worked).
NordGrid’s Runbook Library

rows: Upstream vendor notifies or Bronze validation detects change

blocks.title: The runbook test: can a new engineer follow it at 03:00?
blocks.content: NordGrid tests runbook quality by having the newest team member execute the runbook during a drill – without asking questions. Every ambiguity discovered during a drill is fixed immediately.

Data freshness is the most visible SLI to consumers. NordGrid monitors freshness with a dedicated freshness-check pipeline that runs at 03:30 UTC and fires an alert for any table not refreshed within its SLA.
Freshness Definitions
Freshness Dependencies

Freshness SLAs cascade: the Gold table cannot be fresh if Silver is stale. NordGrid checks tables in pipeline order: Silver first, then Gold, then the semantic model. A Silver freshness breach is severity-1 because it blocks all downstream freshness.

blocks.title: Freshness SLAs must account for the consumer’s schedule
blocks.content: NordGrid’s freshness SLAs are set at the pipeline’s expected completion time plus a buffer, not at the consumer’s usage time. This ensures breaches are detected immediately rather than at consumer impact time.
Individual SLIs measure specific aspects. Platform health metrics measure the overall trajectory: is the platform getting better or worse over time?
Monthly Health Metrics

blocks.title: Slow decline is harder to detect than sudden failure
blocks.content: A pipeline that takes 14 minutes today and 14.3 next month does not trigger any alert. But the trend crosses the 20-minute SLA in 18 months if unchecked. Platform health metrics detect this slow decline because they track trends, not just thresholds.
A change to any one component can ripple through others: a Silver schema change affects the Gold stored procedure, the SQL views, the semantic model, and potentially the billing team’s automation.
The Change Request Template
Every change to a production item begins with a change request: what is changing, why, what is the impact, what is the risk, what is the rollback plan, and when will it be deployed.
Impact Analysis Using Lineage
Fabric’s lineage view shows the data flow from source to consumer. The engineer traces lineage to identify every item that needs updating and every team that needs notification.
The Change Advisory Board

High-impact changes go through a weekly 30-minute CAB meeting where team leads review proposed changes, agree on timing, and assign communication owners. NordGrid’s CAB prevents surprises: no consumer team discovers a schema change by encountering a broken dashboard.

blocks.title: Communicate before you deploy, not after
blocks.content: Consumer teams must be notified at least 48 hours before a high-impact change reaches production. A deployment that surprises consumers erodes trust – even if technically correct.
Fabric is a cloud platform that evolves continuously. NordGrid’s platform must evolve with it: adopting new features, migrating away from deprecated patterns, and absorbing breaking changes.
Evaluating New Features

NordGrid’s three-question framework: (1) Does this feature solve a current problem? (2) What is the adoption cost? (3) What is the risk? Adopt GA features that solve current problems; evaluate preview features for future planning but do not deploy them to production.
The Annual Platform Roadmap
Every January, NordGrid produces an annual roadmap with three sections: near-term (Q1–Q2) features to adopt now, mid-term (Q3) features to pilot, and long-term (Q4+) features to monitor.
Handling Deprecations

NordGrid follows the strangler fig pattern: build the new implementation alongside the old one, validate identical results, switch the pipeline, and decommission the old after a 30-day monitoring period.

blocks.title: Evolution is maintenance, not innovation
blocks.content: Most evolution work is maintenance: keeping the platform current with cloud platform changes. NordGrid budgets 20% of engineering capacity for evolution work – preventing technical debt from accumulating.

sections.title: What Comes Next – The Senior Track

blocks.content: The mid-level track taught you to build and operate a production Fabric platform for a 3-team, 15-person organization. The senior-level track scales to GridUnion Continental – a multi-country utility with 5 million meters, 20,000 turbines, 12 teams, and 80 engineers.
Senior-Level Topics and Their Mid-Level Foundations

rows: Separate business units with independent data, shared infrastructure
Complex event processing, multi-stream correlation, exactly-once semantics
Custom UDFs, broadcast variable strategies, memory-pressure debugging
Self-service data platforms, internal data products
Multi-table transactions, change data capture patterns

blocks.title: The mid-level track is the foundation, not the ceiling
blocks.content: Every skill in the mid-level track – execution plans, Delta operations, pipeline patterns, Warehouse design, real-time intelligence, testing, CI/CD, capacity management, governance – applies at senior scale. The senior track does not replace these skills; it builds on them.

sections.title: From Pipeline Builder to Platform Engineer

blocks.content: Ten modules. One hundred lessons. From Spark execution plans to platform evolution planning. The transition is not about knowing more – it is about thinking differently about what ‘done’ means. At junior level, done means the pipeline works. At mid-level, done means the pipeline works, is tested, is deployed through CI/CD, is monitored for SLA compliance, is governed for security and compliance, is documented with runbooks, and has a plan for evolution.
The NordGrid Platform Today

NordGrid’s Fabric platform processes 1.2 million meter readings and 43 million SCADA telemetry events daily. It serves 3 teams with 15 people across 13 workspaces on F64 + F32 capacities. The Silver transformation completes in 14 minutes with 30% SLA headroom. The CI/CD pipeline deploys to production 3 times per week with zero direct-edit incidents. The test suite runs 45 tests in 15 seconds and has prevented 12 production incidents in 6 months.
The Mid-Level Promise

You now have the skills to tune Spark performance, operate Delta tables with MERGE and maintenance, build resilient pipelines, design Warehouse tables, build real-time streaming, test systematically, deploy through CI/CD, manage capacity and cost, govern a multi-team environment, and operate the entire platform with SLIs, monitoring, and incident management.

blocks.title: The platform is the product
blocks.content: NordGrid’s three consumer teams interact with fresh data in the Warehouse, live metrics on the operations dashboard, and certified items in the OneLake catalog. The platform – the invisible machinery that produces this experience – is the product. When consumers stop thinking about the platform and start thinking only about the data, the platform engineer has succeeded.
